seats_text: 座敷あり（個室なし）。具体的な席数は非公開。
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00（食べログ記載）。来訪ブログでは11:00-18:00との記載もあり、夜営業は要問い合わせ。定休日：水曜。
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running family udon shop catering mostly to local and Japanese tourists; English support is unconfirmed, so go with a guide or a Japanese-speaking companion and bring written allergy cards.
vegan_detail: No vegan options identifiable. The signature Ise udon uses a fish-based dashi tare, tekone sushi is marinated bonito (katsuo) over rice, and set meals include egg (tamagoyaki), fish, and seafood sides. No vegetable-only or plant-based dishes are advertised.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac. The core dish is wheat udon, and the Ise udon tare is built on tamari/soy sauce (the shop is described as using tamari-shoyu dashi handed down ~50 years) plus fish dashi — soy sauce here typically contains wheat. Tekone sushi fish is marinated in a soy-based sauce. Tempura (karaage/tempura udon) is on the menu, indicating a shared fryer; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free menu exists. No allergy program documented.

bio.background: A family-run teahouse-style udon restaurant that has operated for roughly 50 years a short walk from Toba Station, near the aquarium. It is regarded locally as a go-to spot for Ise udon, with the shop's dashi tare passed down across generations using tamari-style soy sauce.
bio.specialty: Ise udon — thick, soft noodles simmered long and dressed in a dark, gently sweet soy-and-fish tare — and tekone sushi, soy-marinated bonito over vinegared rice with shredded egg, seaweed, and shiso. The popular set pairs both with a sauce blending soy, sake, and sugar.
bio.philosophy: Carry forward a homestyle, generations-deep recipe for Ise udon and the regional fisherman's dish of tekone sushi.

anecdotes.text: Tekone sushi was reportedly added for customers who felt udon alone was not enough, becoming a signature regional pairing alongside the noodles.

A 2025 visitor during Golden Week described the recommended set with soy-marinated bonito tekone sushi over shiso-flecked rice, plus thick Ise udon boiled around 40 minutes for a soft, smooth texture in a subtly sweet fish-dashi tare.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and karaage (fried) items appear on the menu, indicating a shared fryer; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Ise udon tare is described as a tamari/soy-sauce-based dashi handed down ~50 years; tekone sushi fish is marinated in a soy-based sauce. Standard soy sauce contains wheat.
A visitor confirmed the tekone sushi as bonito marinated in soy-based tare and the udon tare as a fish-dashi, gently sweet soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ise udon tare uses fish dashi; tekone sushi is marinated bonito (katsuo); set meals include egg and seafood sides. No plant-only dishes documented.

name_ja: ミュゼア コーヒー 鳥羽駅店
cuisine: カフェ・自家焙煎コーヒー・スパイスカレー・ベーカリー
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering identified. This is a café and bakery whose signature items are bread-based — freshly baked bread, panini, and the popular spice curry bread (カレーパン) — all of which contain wheat. The lunch spice curry set is served with bread/salad and the roux base is not confirmed GF (most Japanese curry uses wheat flour; spice curries may not, but unconfirmed here). No celiac-safe handling, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free menu is documented. A celiac should treat this as unsafe absent direct confirmation from staff.
vegan_detail: No declared vegan menu. Coffee can be ordered black (self-roasted, hand-drip) and is plant-friendly, but the food program centers on bread, baked goods (cookies, langue de chat — typically butter/egg), soft-serve ice cream (dairy), café latte (dairy), and spice curry of unverified composition. Beef stroganoff at the main store is clearly non-vegan. Curry may contain dashi/meat stock — unconfirmed. A vegan diner would be limited to black coffee unless staff can confirm specific items.

neighborhood: 近鉄・JR鳥羽駅構内（駅1階）
seats_text: 18 seats; entirely non-smoking; no private rooms; no parking.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (open Mon, Wed–Sun); closed Tuesdays. Note: the MUSEA official site lists the Toba Station branch as 10:00-16:00 open year-round — hours may vary; confirm before a late-afternoon visit.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual in-station coffee stand and bakery counter; ordering is simple but expect Japanese-only service, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.

bio.background: MUSEA began in December 2013 as a specialty coffee roastery and café in the seaside town of Toba, and has since grown to several locations including a flagship general-store-and-café, this compact stand inside Toba Station, a counter at the entrance to Toba Aquarium, and an outpost in Nagoya's Osu district. The Toba Station branch opened on March 7, 2021.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee served hand-drip, alongside the shop's popular spice curry bread (カレーパン) and freshly baked breads; at lunch the brand is known for a spice curry set served with soup and salad.
bio.philosophy: The brand's idea is 'a special cup of coffee that feels the sea breeze' — beans from around the world are roasted on-site at their oceanside roastery in Toba, with an emphasis on freshness and careful hand-drip preparation.

anecdotes.text: The flagship MUSEA is a two-floor space of modern concrete design with curated art, retail merchandise and bay views from the upper seating — a setting praised by visitors for its strong aesthetic sense.
MUSEA roasts its beans at a roastery by the sea in Toba, framing each cup as something that lets you 'feel the sea breeze.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working bakery whose signature products are wheat-based bread, panini and spice curry bread, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination likely; no GF accommodation is documented.

positives.text: Self-roasted coffee can be ordered black (hand-drip), giving a reliably plant-based, gluten-free beverage option.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00 closed Tuesdays, while the MUSEA official site lists the Toba Station branch as 10:00-16:00 open year-round. No gluten-free or vegan menu is documented; dietary suitability beyond black coffee is unconfirmed and should be checked with staff. Spice-curry roux composition (wheat flour / dashi / meat stock) is not stated in any fetched source.

cuisine: カフェ・喫茶（自家焙煎コーヒー、スパイスカレー、ワッフル、パニーニ、軽食）
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published by the shop or any third-party source. This is a café whose core menu leans wheat-heavy: spice curry, beef stroganoff, paninis, sandwiches, house-baked bread, waffles and cakes all typically contain wheat flour. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer, separate prep area, or any cross-contamination controls, and the kitchen handles flour throughout. A celiac diner should assume the kitchen is not gluten-safe and must ask directly about every dish; house-roasted black coffee is naturally GF but may be prepared in a shared, flour-dusted environment. Treat as ask/likely-no until confirmed in person.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. Signature savory dishes (spice curry, beef stroganoff, roast beef rice) contain meat, and curries/soups in Japan commonly use animal- or fish-based stock (dashi, bonito/katsuo, meat broth) — none of which is disclosed here. Baked goods, waffles and cakes likely contain dairy and eggs. The clearest plant-based option is black self-roasted coffee or tea, but milk-free preparation and any plant-milk availability are unconfirmed. A vegan diner must ask about hidden dashi/katsuo and dairy/egg before ordering.

bio.background: MUSEA opened on December 30, 2013 in the seaside town of Toba, Mie, as a combined general store and café. The first floor is a retail space selling house-roasted coffee beans and lifestyle goods, while the second floor is a light-filled café with bay views — a single building that doubles as shop and gathering place.
bio.specialty: House-roasted coffee paired with a fragrant spice curry plate; the café is also known for its house-made waffle plates and seafood/spice curry, served with complimentary soup refills and salad at lunch.
bio.philosophy: Carefully roasted coffee and thoughtfully made food in a considered space — the interior, location and details reflect a strong owner's sensibility, with the building's modern concrete second floor designed by architect Teppei Fujiwara.

anecdotes.text: What began as a single general-store café in 2013 has grown into a small Toba family of MUSEA spots — the flagship store, a stand at Kintetsu Toba Station, a location at Toba Aquarium, and an experimental Hinoyama Lab.

The flagship's airy second-floor café, with its modern concrete design and views over the bay, was created by architect Teppei Fujiwara, giving the everyday coffee-and-curry stop an unexpectedly refined setting.

cuisine: Seafood / Japanese-Western (signature opened-shrimp fry, sashimi, kaiseki)
neighborhood: Toba 3-chōme, near Toba Station
gf_detail: This is a fried-shrimp specialist. The signature 開き海老フライ (opened shrimp fry) is coated in wheat flour, egg, and breadcrumbs (panko) before frying — explicitly stated by the kitchen — so it is unsafe for celiacs and there is no GF version. The fryer is shared, presenting cross-contamination risk for any other item. Sets come with miso soup (typically dashi/miso, often containing barley miso and bonito) and pickles. No gluten-free menu, no allergen labeling, and no dedicated fryer were found on the official site, the menu page, or any source. Treat as not gluten-free safe.
vegan_detail: A seafood house: the menu is built around fried shrimp, sashimi, fish-offal stew, and kaiseki sets with grilled fish and Japanese beef. Miso soup and sauces almost certainly rest on bonito/fish dashi, and the tartar sauce contains egg. No vegetarian or vegan dish was identified on the official menu page. Not suitable for vegans.

seats_text: Around 60 seats; a sizeable sit-down restaurant that gets very busy at peak times.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon, Wed–Fri): 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 16:30–19:30 (L.O. 18:30). Sat–Sun: 10:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 16:30–19:30 (L.O. 18:30). Holidays / peak season: 10:30–19:30 (L.O. 18:30). May close earlier if dishes sell out; the limited large-shrimp set (20/day) often sells out.
closed_days: Tuesdays (open if a public holiday falls on Tuesday, with a substitute closure); occasional temporary closures for equipment maintenance.
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, tourist-familiar local institution where pointing at the picture menu works well, but staff communication is mainly in Japanese, so a few key phrases or a translation app help.

bio.background: A long-standing Toba seafood restaurant a short walk from the station, marked by its big 漣 signboard, with its own parking lot across the road. It draws long lines in season, getting through as much as 60kg of shrimp on a busy day.
bio.specialty: 開き海老フライ — the 'opened' butterflied shrimp fry. Large, fresh shrimp are split open, given a light coat of flour, egg, and panko, and fried to a crisp, thick, bite-y texture, then served with the house tartar sauce and a wedge of Mie-grown Meyer lemon. The style of opening the shrimp before frying is said to have originated here.
bio.philosophy: Head chef Hayashi, Tokyo-trained and at the helm for well over a decade, sums it up as simple, delicious cooking that lets the ingredients speak: "素材を活かすシンプルで美味しい料理が売りです".

anecdotes.text: The butterflied 'opened shrimp fry' served here is credited as the origin of the style: "海老を開いてから揚げる海老フライは『漣』発祥と言われています".
On busy days the kitchen goes through as much as 60kg of shrimp, and the daily large-shrimp set is capped at 20 servings, which sell out fast.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature fried shrimp is coated in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the fryer carries wheat; no separate GF preparation was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and Japanese sets are served with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat unless a tamari alternative is requested; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every set includes miso soup and the kitchen centers on fish and shellfish, so fish/bonito dashi and shared equipment make vegan separation impractical.

positives.text: Simple, ingredient-forward cooking and a picture-friendly set menu make it easy to see exactly what you are ordering.

caveats: Real, operating, and well within the target radius (~0.2km from Toba Station). Strongly omnivore/seafood-focused: the house specialty is wheat-breaded deep-fried shrimp, so it is not suitable for celiacs or vegans. No allergen menu, GF menu, or dedicated fryer found on any source; GF/vegan diners should not rely on this spot. Founding year and founder name were not confirmed by any source.

cuisine: 海鮮・海鮮丼・郷土料理
gf_detail: No published gluten-free or allergy program. This is a traditional Toba seafood/izakaya house where wheat is pervasive: soy sauce (shoyu) on sashimi, seafood bowls, and tekone sushi almost always contains wheat; the signature Ise udon is a wheat-flour noodle in a wheat-based dark soy/tamari broth; tempura and the famous fried shrimp (ebi fry) are wheat-breaded and fried in shared oil. Dashi for kamameshi, simmered dishes, and udon is typically katsuo/kombu based and may be seasoned with wheat-containing soy. No dedicated fryer and no cross-contamination controls are documented. A celiac would need to ask in detail in Japanese and rely on plain raw sashimi with tamari brought from home; treat as ask-level at best.
vegan_detail: A seafood-centered restaurant with no published vegan or vegetarian options. The kitchen is built around fish, shellfish, and seafood; even rice and noodle dishes (kamameshi, Ise udon) are built on katsuo/bonito and kombu dashi, and soy-based seasonings are common. There is no documented plant-based menu. A strict vegan should not rely on this place without calling ahead; at best ask whether a plain vegetable or rice item can be prepared without fish dashi, which is unlikely to be standard here.

seats_text: 88 seats across three floors (1F: 20, 2F: 32, 3F: 36), with private rooms and a banquet hall accommodating up to about 40.
hours_raw: Tue-Fri, Sun & holidays: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) and 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Sat: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) and 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Mon: closed (open if a holiday, closed the following day instead). Hours may extend during three-day holiday weekends.
closed_days: Mondays (open if a public holiday, closing the following day instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy local seafood house catering mainly to Japanese diners and tour groups; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, and dietary restrictions must be explained clearly since no allergy program exists.

bio.background: Tenbinya is a long-running Toba seafood house just steps from Toba Station, run as part of the Hama-no-Ajidokoro Tenbinya group, which also operates a nearby Ichibangai branch and a grill-focused sister shop, あみ焼き 天びん屋. The kitchen leans on its location in this fishing-port town: as the shop puts it, the bounty of the 'treasure sea' is delivered straight from the harbor to Tenbinya.
bio.specialty: Ama no kamameshi (the sea-diver's clay-pot rice, recognized as an official Toba boxed meal) and a six-topping seafood bowl crowned with sea urchin and salmon roe; lunch also features regional Ise udon and tekone sushi.
bio.philosophy: Hospitality expressed through every element from ingredients and cooking method to the space and service, built around fresh local 'iso' (rocky-shore) seafood.

anecdotes.text: The signature ama no kamemeshi is certified as an official Toba bento, tying the menu to the region's tradition of female free-divers (ama).
The seafood bowl is built from six kinds of fresh fish topped with both uni (sea urchin) and ikura (salmon roe).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes wheat-breaded fried shrimp (ebi fry) and tempura, indicating shared fryer oil with no documented dedicated gluten-free fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seafood bowls, sashimi, and tekone sushi are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is advertised.
Regional Ise udon is a wheat noodle served in a dark soy-based sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is centered on fish and shellfish, and rice/noodle dishes such as kamameshi and Ise udon are typically prepared with katsuo (bonito)/kombu dashi; no plant-based menu is published.

caveats: No published allergy/gluten/vegan program; GF and vegan assessments are inferred from the seafood/izakaya menu and standard Japanese kitchen practice and should be confirmed directly with the restaurant. Tabelog listed a slightly broader seat count (88) than the official site, which emphasizes private rooms and a ~40-person banquet hall.

cuisine: 居酒屋・焼貝・海鮮 (grilled-shellfish & seafood izakaya)
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（さざえストリート・鳥羽駅周辺）
gf_detail: Naturally lower-risk core concept: shellfish (sazae/turban shell, large clams, hioki clams, abalone, Ise lobster) and Toshima-jima shirasu are grilled or served plainly, and shell-on charcoal grilling means much of the food is just the shellfish itself. However, this is a traditional izakaya with no documented allergy or gluten-free program. Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) and dipping sauces are typically offered for grilled shellfish and sashimi, and izakaya kitchens routinely use shared surfaces and seasonings. A celiac would need to ask explicitly for items grilled/served with no soy sauce or sauce, and confirm seasoning on the shirasu and any cooked dishes. No dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol confirmed. Treat as "ask in advance."
vegan_detail: A seafood/grilled-shellfish specialist whose menu is almost entirely shellfish, raw fish, and seafood; there is no documented plant-based main. Even where vegetables might appear, broths and seasonings at a seafood izakaya commonly rely on katsuo/bonito and fish-based dashi. No vegan options found.

seats_text: About 30 seats: counter seating plus tables for 2 and 4, with half-private rooms that can hold 10–20 for groups.
hours_raw: 10:15-18:30 (L.O. 18:00, last reservation 17:45). Open daily; closures are irregular (不定期).
closed_days: No fixed closing day; closures are irregular (不定期). Hours are daytime/early-evening (closes 18:30), so confirm before a late visit.
cultural_comfort_note: A local, family-run shellfish counter where you pick from the live tank and staff grill in front of you; basic Japanese (or pointing at the tank) goes a long way, as English support is not advertised.
caveats: Daytime-only izakaya (closes 18:30); reservations are advised and a cancellation fee (around ¥1,000/person) may apply. No gluten-free or vegan program is documented — celiac and vegan diners should confirm details directly before relying on it.

bio.background: A small, locally loved shellfish counter on Toba's Sazae Street, just a short walk from Toba Station. The heart of the place is a live tank of seasonal shellfish: you choose what looks good, and the staff grill it in front of you over charcoal, shell and all, so the catch comes to the table piping hot.
bio.specialty: Grilled local shellfish to order — turban shell (sazae), large clams (oh-asari), abalone, and the prized hioki clam, a Toba specialty that looks like a scallop but carries a deeper, richer umami. A standout is the fluffy shirasu (whitebait) from nearby Toshima Island, eaten with ginger.
bio.philosophy: Let the day's catch speak for itself: simple charcoal grilling, shell-on, so diners taste the sea directly, paired with local Mie sake and a relaxed daytime-drinking welcome.

anecdotes.text: You pick your shellfish straight from the live tank and the staff grill it before your eyes; the hioki clam is singled out as a distinctly Toba treat, scallop-like in looks but far richer in flavor.
Regulars note it is a relaxed, locals' spot on Sazae Street that even welcomes guests dining with their dogs by advance reservation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional seafood izakaya, grilled shellfish and sashimi are commonly served with or dipped in standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers entirely on shellfish and seafood, so any shared surfaces, grills, and broths should be assumed to involve fish/shellfish.

positives.text: Much of the menu is whole shellfish grilled shell-on over charcoal, so the core food is naturally simple and unbreaded — a diner who skips soy/sauce can ask for it plain.

lead: あみ焼き 天びん屋
name_ja: あみ焼き 天びん屋
cuisine: 海鮮・焼肉・あみ焼き
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Grill-your-own seafood and meat house. Lower-risk: oyster gangan-yaki, plain grilled shellfish, grilled vegetables eaten without sauce. Grilled meats use soy-based tare or miso (typically wheat); menu has shrimp tempura and fried items; shared grills/tongs create cross-contact. No dedicated fryer documented. Ask-and-verify.
vegan_detail: A seafood and meat grill, not vegan-oriented. Only plant items are grilled vegetables, cooked on grills shared with oysters/shellfish/meat; sauces likely contain bonito/dashi. A vegan could manage only plain vegetables on a clean surface after confirming.
geocode_note: GSI returned [136.842957, 34.484089] for the address, within the target box. About 0.3km from Toba Station.

seats_text: 52 seats total — counter (4), plus tables and tatami/zashiki seating, with private rooms available.
closed_days: Tuesdays and Thursdays (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A local seafood-grill house oriented to Japanese diners and tourists; expect Japanese-language service and a hands-on grill-your-own format.

bio.background: Amiyaki Tenbinya is one of several seafood houses run under the Tenbinya name in the bayside town of Toba, where the kitchens build their cooking around the day's local catch. This branch is the grill-it-yourself sibling of the main Tenbinya restaurant, near Toba Station and the working harbor.
bio.specialty: Oyster gangan-yaki: local Uramura oysters piled into a large tin can and steamed open at the table, served year-round, alongside grilled spiny lobster, scallops, clams, abalone, plus Matsusaka beef and Ise young chicken grilled at the table.
bio.philosophy: A rustic, bayside way of eating: bold, informal seafood cooked over fire in front of you, leaning on Toba's harbor and the region's prized meats.

anecdotes.text: The house signature is gangan-yaki, where oysters are loaded into a large can and sake-steamed open at the table; the kitchen keeps it available all year by sourcing peak-season oysters in late winter and deep-freezing them at about -60C.
Beyond oysters, the grill turns out Ise young chicken and A4-grade Matsusaka beef finished with the house miso-based sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Grill-your-own format with shared grills and tongs creates cross-contact between seafood, sauced meats, and naturally GF items; no separate surface documented.
Menu includes shrimp tempura and other fried items; no dedicated fryer mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled meats served with soy-based tare or miso sauce, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Grilled vegetables cooked on grills shared with oysters, shellfish, and meat.

positives.text: Naturally simpler items can be eaten plain: oyster gangan-yaki, grilled shellfish, grilled vegetables.

lead: うどん・そば 吉平
cuisine: うどん・そば（伊勢うどん／麺類）
neighborhood: 鳥羽・鳥羽駅 徒歩圏内（鳥羽一番街 3F）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Ise udon — thick wheat-flour noodles — served in a dark, concentrated tamari/soy-style tare that contains wheat. Soba dishes are listed but are very unlikely to be 100% buckwheat (typical udon/soba shops blend wheat into soba, and the noodles share a wheat-heavy kitchen). Tempura items (large shrimp, chikuwa) use wheat batter and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer. The broth/tare for noodle dishes is dashi-based; even vegetable-topped bowls (wakame, aonori, akamoku) are served in this wheat-containing tare. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering can be confirmed. Ise udon and the soba/udon broths are built on Japanese dashi, which conventionally uses katsuobushi (bonito) and/or other fish; the signature Ise udon tare is a soy-based sauce typically made with bonito/dried-fish stock. Several toppings are overtly animal-based (Toba whitebait/shirasu, oyster, Matsusaka beef, shrimp tempura, egg in kamatama and oyakodon, katsudon). Vegetable-topped bowls (wakame, fresh aonori, akamoku seaweed) look plant-forward but are served in fish-dashi broth/tare, so they are not vegan, and no dashi-free or vegan preparation is advertised. The page itself notes items may contain dashi or seafood-derived broths. Treat as not suitable for vegans; would need to ask directly and likely cannot be accommodated.

bio.origin: 鳥羽市（鳥羽一番街・鳥羽駅前）
bio.background: 麺処 吉平 is a casual local noodle shop on the third floor of 鳥羽一番街, the souvenir-and-dining complex right beside Toba Station, serving travelers and locals who come off the trains and ferries.
bio.specialty: Ise udon — the area's famous thick, soft, chewy wheat noodles in a dark, mellow tamari-style tare — including a kamatama version topped with locally landed Toba shirasu (whitebait), a large-shrimp-tempura bowl, and seasonal Ise-Shima toppings like winter oysters with fresh aonori, summer sudachi, akamoku seaweed, and Matsusaka beef.
bio.philosophy: 地産地消 — the shop leans on local Ise-Shima ingredients and seasonal toppings to put a regional spin on the classic comfort dish of Ise udon.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits on the 3rd floor of 鳥羽一番街 directly next to Toba Station, making it a convenient first or last stop for visitors to the bay.
Its menu rotates with the seasons of Ise-Shima — winter brings oyster-and-aonori bowls, summer brings a chilled sudachi udon — alongside the year-round Toba shirasu kamatama Ise udon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is wheat-heavy — wheat-flour Ise udon noodles, likely wheat-blended soba, and battered tempura are all prepared in the same space, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ise udon is defined by its dark, concentrated tamari/soy-style tare; standard versions contain wheat-derived soy sauce, and no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths/tare are dashi-based and the menu notes items may contain dashi or seafood-derived broths, so even vegetable-topped bowls (wakame, aonori, akamoku) carry fish stock.

caveats: Official site lists hours and Thursday/irregular closures but not seat count; Tabelog and the official site do not state dashi composition explicitly, so the bonito-dashi inference follows standard Ise udon / udon-soba practice rather than an on-page statement. No gluten-free or vegan information is published, and no allergy-handling claims could be confirmed.

The core menu is wheat-heavy: Ise udon and the signature "Toba SOBA" are wheat-flour noodles, and sandwiches (aosa egg, Ise pork cutlet, Ise red chicken salad) use bread and breaded/fried cutlets. No source mentions allergen labeling, gluten-free items, separate prep, or a dedicated fryer. For a celiac this should be treated as not safe unless staff can confirm otherwise in person.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Most savory items contain egg, dairy, pork, chicken, or fish/seaweed broths. Plant-leaning items that exist (matcha/hojicha lattes, drip coffee, fruit cube sandwiches, shaved ice) typically involve dairy milk, and the aosa "egg" sandwich contains egg. Noodle broths likely use katsuo/bonito dashi (unconfirmed). Nothing is confirmed vegan, so a vegan diner would need to ask carefully about each item; assume hidden dashi and dairy by default.
caveats: Premise of "GF・ベジ相談しやすい" in the brief is NOT supported by any fetched source — no allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented for this venue. Treat dietary safety as unconfirmed/ask-in-person. Official standalone website not found (covered via Tabelog, tourism, and listing pages). Seat count and "no closed days" come from a station-cafe listing and may shift seasonally.

bio.background: かもめベイテラス鳥羽 sits just outside the ticket gates on the second floor of Kintetsu Toba Station, opening as a renewed concept on July 1, 2021 (following an earlier run as かもめテラス鳥羽). It is a station-side cafe built to give travelers a first or last taste of Ise-Shima before they board the train, with terrace-style seating overlooking the Toba townscape and bay.
bio.specialty: "Toba SOBA" — a new local noodle dish topped with island seaweed such as aosa and akamoku, served alongside classic Ise udon and bite-size cube sandwiches made with local ingredients like Ise Bay aosa, Ise-Umaine pork, and Ise red chicken.
bio.philosophy: Showcase the appeal of Ise-Shima through locally sourced ingredients while staying plastic-free: paper cups and straws, wooden spoons, sugarcane-fiber (bagasse) bowls, and plant-based compostable cups.

anecdotes.text: The drinks lean local and made-to-order: matcha and hojicha lattes use Ise tea leaves from a regional tea garden, and the drip coffee is brewed in-house from beans roasted by a nearby Meiwa-town roaster.
A summer-only shaved ice called "Rias Ice" is cut into delicate strands meant to evoke the rippling coastal waves of the Rias coastline, offered in flavors like strawberry-milk and matcha-milk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ise udon is served in a dark tare typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published, so wheat in the seasoning should be assumed.

positives.text: Casual, takeout-friendly cafe where you can clearly see and choose individual items (cube sandwiches, noodles, drinks), making it easier to point to and ask about specific ingredients in person.

lead: ブランカ 鳥羽本店

vegan_detail: ヴィーガン不可。主力の洋菓子はバター（乳製品）と卵を基本とした焼き菓子で、シェルレーヌも卵白・北海道産バターを使用します。植物性のみのヴィーガン対応商品は確認できませんでした。

geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.842972, 34.481007] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-15-3, inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). Roughly 50–300m from Toba Station; consistent with the shop's stated ~4-minute walk from Toba Station south exit.

cultural_comfort_note: 気軽に立ち寄れる地元の老舗洋菓子店で、簡単な日本語の挨拶があれば購入はスムーズですが、英語対応やアレルギー個別相談は期待しにくいので注意が必要です。
caveats: グルテンフリー・ヴィーガンいずれにも非対応。小麦・卵・乳を主原料とする洋菓子店のため、セリアックやヴィーガンの方が食べられる商品は確認できませんでした。掲載は主に「同行者の購入先・お土産スポット」としての参考情報です。

bio.background: 鳥羽駅からほど近い鳥羽市鳥羽に本店を構える、地元に根ざした老舗の洋菓子店。三重・伊勢志摩を代表する菓子づくりの会社として知られています。
bio.specialty: 看板商品は「シェルレーヌ」。アコヤ真珠貝をかたどった貝殻型の焼き菓子（マドレーヌ系）で、外はサックリ、中はしっとりとした食感に、焦がしバターのコクとアーモンドの香りが広がります。地元三重県産小麦「あやひかり」、地元産の卵、バター、そしてアコヤ真珠貝の内層から作る天然の「真珠貝カルシウム」を使い、職人が一つひとつ手焼きしています。
bio.philosophy: 膨張剤や保存料などの添加物を一切使わず、バター・卵・小麦粉といった素材本来のおいしさを味わってもらうことを大切にしています。

anecdotes.text: 看板の「シェルレーヌ」はG7伊勢志摩サミットでコーヒーブレイクの菓子として供され、各国首脳に振る舞われました。
第22回全国菓子大博覧会で名誉総裁賞、ジャパン・ギフト・アワード2017で三重賞を受賞しています。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 看板商品シェルレーヌの原材料に小麦粉が含まれ、焼き菓子全般が小麦ベース。グルテンフリー製品や専用調理の記載はなく、共有設備での製造とみられます。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 主力商品はバター（乳）と卵を使用する焼き菓子で、植物性のみの商品は確認できませんでした。

positives.text: シェルレーヌの特定原材料（小麦・乳・卵・アーモンド）が明記されており、含有アレルゲンを事前に確認できます。

cuisine: 純喫茶・喫茶店（軽食・パスタ・カレー・ご当地バーガー「とばーがー」）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era coffee shop (junkissa, est. 1976) built around wheat-based dishes — spaghetti/pasta, curry served with rice but thickened with wheat-flour roux, sandwiches, cakes/parfaits, and the signature "Tobaburger," which is octopus and sauce served inside a koppe-pan (wheat bread roll). There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer indicated, and no allergen handling described anywhere. Curry roux, soy sauce, and dashi all typically contain wheat. A celiac cannot eat safely here; treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: A retro coffee shop centered on animal products: the famous Tobaburger is octopus (tako from Toshijima) with mayonnaise; menu features shirasu (whitebait) pasta, local-pork pasta, oyster-fry bento, and cake/parfait sets (dairy/egg). Dashi (bonito/katsuo) is standard in Japanese savory cooking here. No vegan items are advertised and no plant-based accommodation is described. Not suitable for vegans.

seats_text: Sit-down coffee shop with table and sofa seating; exact seat count not published.

bio.background: Kissa Cherry has been a fixture inside the Toba Ichibangai building right beside Toba Station since 1976, a genuine Showa-era 'junkissa' (pure coffee shop). It is the kind of nostalgic, unhurried room where travelers settle in over coffee and a plate of spaghetti while waiting for a train or the ferry out to the bay's islands.
bio.specialty: The signature item is the 'Tobaburger' — the shop's take on Toba's local specialty, octopus (tako, associated with nearby Toshijima) dressed in a house-made sauce and tucked into a koppe-pan roll, finished with a nutty topping. It is billed as the most convenient place near Toba Station to try the regional burger, alongside local pasta (whitebait, regional pork) and curry.
bio.philosophy: A retro, calm space that keeps the feel of decades past — somewhere to relax and let time pass before the next train or boat.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1976 and still run in the classic junkissa style, it markets itself as the one spot by Toba Station where you can casually grab the local Tobaburger between connections.
The Tobaburger is an octopus-and-sauce 'go-tochi' burger served in a koppe-pan bun with a nut topping, a playful local riff sold for around 600-780 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on wheat — spaghetti/pasta, sandwiches, cakes and parfaits, plus the Tobaburger served in a wheat koppe-pan roll — in a small shared kitchen with no gluten-free options described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are animal-based (octopus burger with mayo, whitebait pasta, local-pork pasta, oyster fry, dairy/egg cakes and parfaits); no plant-based items advertised.

cuisine: 寿司・活魚・鰻（Sushi, live fish, eel）
neighborhood: 鳥羽（鳥羽駅周辺）
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目3-10
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目3-10 returned [136.841446, 34.483093], within the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). About a 7-minute walk from Toba Station.
seats_text: About 30 seats across counter, tables, and tatami seating.
hours_raw: Sources disagree on exact hours: Tabelog lists 10:00–21:00; Hot Pepper / aggregators list 10:30–19:00 (last order 18:00); the Toba tourism site lists 9:00–20:00 with an irregular schedule. All agree the shop is closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a public holiday) and open through Obon, New Year, and Golden Week.
closed_days: 火曜日（祝日の場合は営業）。お盆・正月・GWは営業。
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running family sushi shop where Japanese will go furthest; there is no sign of English service, so a translation guide or a few Japanese phrases will help, especially for any dietary requests.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is published. As a traditional sushi and cooked-fish shop, the soy sauce used for nigiri and the house-made simmered dishes (nitsuke) almost certainly contain wheat, and unagi/eel kabayaki sauce typically contains wheat as well. There is no dedicated fryer or any stated allergen handling. A celiac diner cannot rely on this place without a direct phone confirmation (0599-26-2045) and a bring-your-own tamari plan; treat as not GF-safe by default.
vegan_detail: This is a fish-centered sushi and katsuo-dashi kitchen; sushi rice, miso, and the house simmered dishes are made with fish/bonito dashi, and the core menu is raw and cooked seafood. No vegan options are advertised. A vegan would have essentially nothing safe here without a special pre-arranged request, and even then hidden dashi (katsuo/bonito) is likely.

bio.background: すし梅 (Sushi Ume) has sold sushi at Toba Port continuously since the early Meiji era (founded 明治初年, around 1868), making it one of the oldest sushi houses in Mie. The shop sits a short walk from Toba Station in the old port district, where it grew up alongside the fishing harbor and built its menu on the local catch.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri and hand-rolled sushi featuring local Toba/Shima fish, plus live fish (kappo) dishes and eel; the kitchen is also known for its house-made simmered seafood (nitsuke).
bio.philosophy: The shop states its aim plainly: 地産地消 (local production for local consumption), and that the simmered dishes are 自家製 (made in-house). It is a neighborhood institution that has fed both Toba locals and visitors for over 150 years.

anecdotes.text: The owner's own words on the shop: "創業明治初年より鳥羽港にて、すし販売を続けております。地産地消をめざし、煮付けは自家製でございます。" — "We have continued selling sushi at Toba Port since the early Meiji era; we aim for local production and consumption, and our simmered dishes are made in-house."

Described as one of the oldest sushi restaurants in Mie Prefecture, popular with both tourists and locals and known for hand-rolled sushi made from local fish, a roughly 10-minute walk from JR/Kintetsu Toba Station.

caveats: Operating hours differ across sources (Tabelog 10:00–21:00; aggregators 10:30–19:00 LO 18:00; tourism site 9:00–20:00 irregular); hours_week is an approximation — confirm before visiting. Closed Tuesdays is consistent across sources. The official domain susiume.com currently presents a TLS certificate that does not match the hostname, so it may show a browser warning. No published allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information exists; all dietary claims must be confirmed by phone (0599-26-2045).

cuisine: 食堂・寿司・海鮮（割烹）— 名物 開き海老フライ
neighborhood: 鳥羽（鳥羽駅周辺）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated anywhere. This is a sushi/seafood kappo where wheat is pervasive: the signature dish is a breaded, deep-fried "ebi fry" (panko + wheat batter), and the kitchen also serves tempura and eel-with-tare — all wheat-based and fried in shared oil, so there is no dedicated fryer and cross-contamination is essentially certain for fried items. Sushi, sashimi and simmered dishes will use standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce, no allergen menu, and no published allergen handling were found. A celiac would need to ask in detail in advance, and even then safety cannot be assured; assume unsafe absent direct confirmation from the restaurant.
vegan_detail: A seafood-centred sushi and kappo kitchen (fish, shrimp, eel, oysters, spiny lobster). Soups and simmered dishes almost certainly rely on bonito (katsuo) dashi, and there is no vegan menu or accommodation indicated. Not a suitable choice for vegans.

hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-20:00（売り切れ次第終了の場合あり）。定休日 木曜（祝日の場合は営業）。
closed_days: Thursdays (open if Thursday is a public holiday). May close early when items sell out.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming local seafood spot popular with residents, but expect a Japanese-only setting and no allergen support, so come prepared to communicate dietary needs in Japanese.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan provisions found; treat as unsafe for celiac (fried wheat items, shared oil, wheat-based soy sauce) and unsuitable for vegans (seafood kitchen, bonito dashi). The "three generations" framing could not be confirmed from any fetched page; the name "Osakaya" reportedly comes from the owner-chef's training period in Osaka. May close early once the day's catch/items sell out.

bio.background: A family-run sushi and kappo restaurant a short walk from Toba Station, beloved by locals for fresh Ise-Shima seafood. The shop reportedly takes its name, "Osakaya," from the years the owner-chef spent training in Osaka before settling in Toba.
bio.specialty: The famous 開き海老フライ — a large butterflied shrimp, breaded and deep-fried so the inside cooks through while staying plump; it is so generous it can be mistaken for aji (horse mackerel) fry until you spot the unmistakable shrimp tail. Sets run from a 2-piece to a 3-piece, and a 'sea' set pairs one ebi fry with sashimi, sushi, a simmered dish and miso soup.
bio.philosophy: Showcase the day's catch from the Ise-Shima waters: live tanks keep seasonal fish and shellfish on hand year-round, with winter oysters and spiny lobster and spring rockfish.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Osakaya' is said to come from the owner-chef's training period in Osaka.
The signature opened ebi fry is so thick and substantial that, breaded, it resembles an aji fry — only the tail gives away that it is shrimp.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature ebi fry is breaded and deep-fried, and the kitchen also fries tempura and serves wheat-based items, all in shared oil — there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A sushi/sashimi kitchen using standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a seafood/sushi kitchen (fish, shrimp, eel, oysters, spiny lobster); soups and simmered dishes almost certainly use bonito dashi, so plant-only dishes cannot be assumed.

cuisine: 海鮮・食堂・居酒屋（地元の魚介、活け簀の貝・伊勢海老）

cultural_comfort_note: A homey, family-run local food hall where a little Japanese goes a long way; staff are warm but English is unlikely, so point at the tank or a photo and a friendly greeting will carry you.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (11:00-17:00 vs 11:00-18:00) and seat count is unconfirmed; call ahead. No gluten-free or vegan provisions are indicated — fried shrimp/oyster batter, soy sauce, and bonito-based dashi (including the complimentary Ise-lobster miso soup) make this a high-risk spot for celiacs and unsuitable for strict vegans without direct confirmation.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated. Signature items include shrimp/oyster fry and tempura (wheat-flour batter), and dishes come with soy sauce and miso soup built on bonito dashi — all standard wheat/cross-contamination risks. There is no mention of a dedicated fryer or allergen handling, so for celiac safety this should be treated as not safe unless verified in person.
vegan_detail: A dedicated seafood restaurant centered on sashimi, shellfish, and Ise lobster. Even side items like miso soup rely on bonito/katsuo dashi, and there are no listed plant-based set meals. Not suitable for vegans without direct confirmation of a specific dish.

bio.background: A small family-run food hall (お食事処) a few minutes' walk south of Toba Station, run by a husband-and-wife pair who serve the day's local catch in a retro, homey room.
bio.specialty: Fresh local seafood straight from a live tank — shellfish and Ise lobster swimming in-store — with hearty set meals; the large fried-shrimp set and assorted-shellfish plates are favorites, and set meals come with a complimentary Ise-lobster miso soup.
bio.philosophy: Generous, reasonably priced plates of seasonal Toba seafood served with personal, neighborly hospitality.

anecdotes.text: Diners repeatedly praise the warm welcome of the proprietor and his wife, describing the place as homey and intimate.
Shellfish and Ise lobster caught that day swim in a tank in the shop, and set meals are served with a complimentary Ise-lobster miso soup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried shrimp and fried oyster set meals are signature dishes, indicating shared fryer use with wheat batter; no dedicated fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A seafood/izakaya menu (sashimi, set meals) where standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used; no gluten-free shoyu indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Set meals include a complimentary Ise-lobster miso soup and the kitchen is built around fish and shellfish, so bonito/seafood dashi is pervasive.

positives.text: Owner couple are known for warm, personal hospitality, which can make it easier to ask about ingredients face to face.

name_ja: ジュビリー ティー&ベイクス
cuisine: 英国紅茶・英国焼き菓子カフェ（個人店）
gf_detail: This is a British-style tea room whose core offering is wheat-based baked goods — scones, Victoria sponge, banoffee pie, apple caramel cake, madeleines (their signature "Coquille Reine"). Cakes, pies and scones are all flour-based, and everything is baked by a solo owner in one kitchen, so cross-contamination with wheat flour is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. Celiac diners should treat this as unsafe; there is no confirmed GF option. Ask the owner directly if you must, but assume wheat throughout.
vegan_detail: A British baking cafe — scones served with Rodda's clotted cream, cakes and pies typically made with butter, eggs and dairy, and the signature madeleine is rich in butter. No vegan menu is documented. Tea itself (Taylors of Harrogate) and the Belvoir rose & raspberry cordial soda are plant-based, so a vegan could likely have a drink, but most baked goods contain dairy/egg. There is no katsuo/bonito dashi concern here since it is a Western tea house, not a Japanese kitchen. Confirm individual items with the owner before ordering food.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目4-65
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.842636, 34.483982] for the chōme-level address; within the target box and adjacent to Toba Station.
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅周辺（鳥羽1丁目）
seats_text: Small intimate tea room; exact seat count not published. Children under 13 are not permitted for eat-in.
hours_raw: 11:30–17:00 (L.O. 16:30)。営業形態が変わっているため要確認。
closed_days: 水曜・木曜（不定休あり。営業形態変更のため要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run tea room where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way; the British theme means menu concepts will feel familiar, but service is in Japanese.
caveats: Business hours/operating format have reportedly changed; confirm directly before visiting. Hours_week reflects the long-published 11:30–17:00 schedule with Wed/Thu closures, but irregular closures occur. Seat count not published. Not suitable for celiac diners; vegans limited to drinks unless an item is confirmed dairy/egg-free.

bio.background: Jubilee Tea & Bakes opened on December 30, 2016, run single-handedly by its female owner. She set out to recreate an authentic British tea-room experience in Toba, sourcing Taylors of Harrogate — a Royal Warrant-holding tea — alongside her own blend coffee made with beans roasted nearby.
bio.specialty: Authentic British scones served with Rodda's clotted cream, plus seasonal cakes and pies — banoffee pie, Victoria sponge, apple caramel cake, lemon-and-sudachi cheesecake and strawberry Eton mess. The signature bake is the shell-shaped, butter-rich madeleine known as the Coquille Reine.
bio.philosophy: A quiet, considered tea room in a renovated traditional house, built around genuine British tea culture and small-batch home baking that changes with the day and the season.

anecdotes.text: The owner first came across the spot while looking for a relaxing place to have coffee on a drive back toward Nagoya, and went on to open her own tea room there.
The shell-shaped Coquille Reine madeleine was served as a tea cake at the 2016 Ise-Shima Summit and was later recognized in the Japan Gift Awards 2017 (Mie prize).

gf_cross_contamination.text: A British bakery where scones, cakes, pies and madeleines are all wheat-flour based and baked by a single owner in one kitchen; wheat cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable and no GF option is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods typically contain butter, eggs and dairy (e.g. scones with Rodda's clotted cream, butter-rich madeleines); no vegan baking is documented.

positives.text: Vegan-friendly drinks are available — Taylors of Harrogate tea and a Belvoir rose & raspberry cordial soda are plant-based.

cuisine: 地産地消の郷土食「鳥羽めし」レストラン＋農水産物の産直市場。地元野菜・地魚・郷土料理を中心としたビュッフェ／定食形式。
gf_detail: グルテンフリー対応の表示・案内は一切確認できない。提供形態はビュッフェ／地産地消の食堂で、天ぷら・かき揚げ・フライなど小麦衣の揚げ物、醤油を使う郷土料理（てこね寿司・煮物）、カレーなど小麦を含む料理が共有の場で多数並ぶ。共有トング・共有油での明確な交差汚染リスクが高く、専用フライヤーの記載もない。セリアック対応は不可と判断。アレルギー情報は店頭/メールでの個別確認が必要（info@tobamarche.jp / 0599-21-1080）。
vegan_detail: 海産物中心の施設で、ヴィーガン／ベジタリアン専用メニューの記載はない。サラダ・お野菜の煮もの・カレーなど野菜料理は並ぶため「菜食寄りに使える」が、煮物・カレー・郷土料理はだし（鰹節）・魚介・醤油を含む可能性が極めて高く、隠れ動物性が想定される。明確に植物性と確認できるのは生野菜サラダや一部のみ。厳格なヴィーガンには事前確認が必須。
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽一丁目2383-42
geocode_note: 国土地理院ジオコーダで住所「三重県鳥羽市鳥羽一丁目2383-42」を検索し、第一フィーチャの座標 [136.843384, 34.487423] を取得。目標ボックス（lat 34.474–34.489 / lng 136.835–136.852）内、鳥羽駅から約0.4km・徒歩約2分で範囲内。
seats_text: レストランは約50席（個室なし、テーブル席中心）。
hours_raw: 産直市場 10:00-18:00（テイクアウト 10:00-17:00）。鳥羽めしレストラン 11:00-13:30（最終受付13:30）。レストランは毎週火曜定休（火曜も市場・直売所は営業）。年末年始（12/31-1/3）休業。
closed_days: 火曜日（レストランのみ。市場・直売所は営業）、年末年始
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅前
cultural_comfort_note: 駅前の観光向け産直市場併設食堂で来訪者に慣れているが、英語メニューやアレルギー個別対応は期待できないため、食事制限がある場合は日本語での事前確認やガイド／指差しが安心。
caveats: 提供形態が2025年2月1日より「地産ビュッフェ」から「鳥羽めしレストラン」へ変更。定休日はタベログ表記（水曜）と公式（火曜）で不一致があり、より新しい公式情報の火曜定休を採用。アレルギー／GF／ヴィーガンに関する公的情報は確認できず、すべて店舗への直接確認が必要。

roles: 鳥羽磯部漁業協同組合（JF）
鳥羽志摩農業協同組合（JA）
bio.origin: 三重県鳥羽市・鳥羽駅前
bio.background: 鳥羽磯部漁協（JF）と鳥羽志摩農協（JA）という、漁業と農業の二つの協同組合が共同で立ち上げた、全国でも珍しい産直施設。地域の一次産業（漁業・農業）と農山漁村の活性化を目的に運営されている。
bio.specialty: 地元の旬の食材を生かした郷土食『鳥羽めし』。地魚の刺身盛り合わせ（一人一皿限定）、てこね寿司、かき揚げ、地魚のカルパッチョなど、海の幸を中心とした地産地消料理。
bio.philosophy: 「健康」と「伝統」をテーマに、生産者の笑顔と収穫の喜びを食の楽しみへ、旬の食材の健康価値を皆さんの元気へ、そして鳥羽の食文化と地域の魅力を食を通して伝えることを掲げる。

anecdotes.text: 漁協と農協が手を組んで運営するという全国的にも珍しい形態で、海産物の直売コーナー（約310㎡）と地物食材のレストランが一体となっている。
地元産いちご（レッドパール）を使ったソフトクリームなど、テイクアウトの名物もある。
2025年2月1日より、それまでの食べ放題ビュッフェから、地元食材を生かした『鳥羽めし』を提供する新スタイルへ転換した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 提供形態が産直市場併設の食堂／ビュッフェ系で、天ぷら・かき揚げ・フライなど小麦衣の揚げ物が共有の場・共有油で多数提供される。共有トングや配膳での交差汚染リスクが高い。
専用フライヤーやグルテンフリー対応に関する記載はどの公式ページにも見当たらない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: てこね寿司（醤油漬けの地魚を使う郷土料理）や煮物など、小麦を含む通常の醤油を使う料理が並ぶ。

vegan_cross_contact.text: お野菜の煮もの・カレー・グラタン・郷土料理にはだし（鰹節）・魚介・乳・醤油など動物性が含まれる可能性が高く、海産物中心の施設のため共有配膳での魚介の混入も想定される。

staff_allergy_handling.text: よくある質問・公式各ページにアレルギー、グルテン、ベジタリアン／ヴィーガン対応の記載はなく、対応可否は確認できない。個別対応はメール／電話での事前問い合わせが必要（info@tobamarche.jp / 0599-21-1080）。

positives.text: サラダ、お野菜の煮もの、刺身、天婦羅、フライ、グラタン、カレーなど25種類以上の地産地消料理が並び、野菜料理の品数はあるため菜食寄りの選択は一定可能。
地元の農産物・水産物の産直市場が併設され、生鮮野菜や果物をその場で購入できる。

lead: 海女小屋 鳥羽 はまなみ
name_ja: 海女小屋 鳥羽 はまなみ
cuisine: 海鮮・海女小屋焼き（さざえ・貝焼き、伊勢えび・あわび、手こね寿司、海鮮丼、海老フライ）
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅山側（鳥羽駅 徒歩約4〜5分）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated anywhere. This is a traditional Toba seafood / ama-hut grill shop where wheat is pervasive: the signature tekone-zushi uses soy-sauce-marinated bonito (Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat), and sashimi/grilled dishes are eaten with soy sauce. Fried items (ebi-fry, large shrimp fry sets) are breaded in wheat panko and fried, with no indication of a dedicated fryer — high cross-contamination risk for a celiac. Dashi/seasonings are unspecified and likely contain wheat-based soy or mirin. No allergen menu, no labeling, no English allergen info. Celiac diners should treat this as not safe unless they can verify specific dishes in person, and even then most of the menu (soy-based, fried, sauced) is off-limits.
vegan_detail: Effectively no vegan options. The entire menu is built around seafood (spiny lobster, abalone, turban shell/sazae, fresh fish sashimi, fried shrimp) and the local tekone-zushi is made with bonito. Dashi and broths almost certainly use bonito/fish stock. There are no listed vegetable-only or plant-based sets, and the ama-hut concept itself is centered on grilled shellfish and seafood. A vegan would not have a viable meal here.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-6-18
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [136.844376, 34.484577] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-6-18, inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and roughly 360m from Toba Station. Note: target brief said 鳥羽3丁目, but the confirmed address (Tabelog, official site, blog) is 鳥羽1丁目; location still well within the 0.8km radius.
seats_text: About 50 seats (roughly 32 table seats and 18 tatami/zashiki seats); mix of tables and family-friendly tatami areas, plus rustic wooden-stump tables near the open kitchen.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon–Wed) 11:00–14:00; Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00–20:00; closed Thursdays and Fridays. Charcoal-grilling course temporarily suspended due to staff shortages.
closed_days: Thursdays and Fridays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, local Japanese seafood shop with no English allergen support and a cash-only, no-reservation policy, so a little Japanese and flexibility help — but for strict GF/vegan needs it is not a workable choice.

bio.background: Hamanami sits just a few minutes' walk up the hill from Toba Station, in the heart of Mie's famed diving coast. It recreates the spirit of the ama-goya — the huts where the region's female free-divers (ama) traditionally warmed themselves and grilled the day's catch over charcoal between dives. The storefront keeps live tanks of fish and shellfish, and the dining room mixes ordinary tables with tatami and rough wooden-stump seats near the open kitchen, giving it an unpretentious, retro Showa-era feel.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — the ama-diving coast of the Shima peninsula
bio.specialty: Grilled shellfish in the ama-hut style (sazae turban shell, abalone, scallops) alongside the local Shima specialty tekone-zushi — bonito marinated in soy sauce laid over sushi rice; also generous sashimi sets featuring spiny lobster and abalone, seafood bowls, and crisp fried shrimp.
bio.philosophy: Serve fresh local Toba seafood simply and generously, in the warm, casual atmosphere of a diver's hut rather than a formal restaurant.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe a cheerful, unpretentious place with live fish tanks at the entrance and a gracious proprietor; one visitor's table was a rustic wooden stump set near the kitchen with a direct view of the cooking.
The kitchen recreates the ama-goya seating where guests could once enjoy charcoal-grilled live spiny lobster and abalone, though the charcoal-grilling course is noted as temporarily suspended.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items such as the ebi-fry and large shrimp-fry sets are breaded (wheat panko) and deep-fried; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is indicated, so shared-oil cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tekone-zushi is made with bonito marinated in soy sauce, and sashimi/grilled dishes are served with soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and there is no gluten-free soy sauce mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is entirely seafood-based (shellfish, fish, shrimp) and the tekone-zushi uses bonito; broths and dashi almost certainly use fish stock, so there is no realistic plant-based or bonito-free option.

caveats: Target brief listed the address as 鳥羽3丁目, but the confirmed address from Tabelog, the official site, and a blog is 鳥羽1-6-18 (still within the 0.8km radius of Toba Station). Charcoal-grilling course is temporarily suspended due to staff shortages; the shop itself remains open. Menu prices vary by source/season (sets roughly ¥1,350–¥2,300). No allergen labeling, GF, or vegan provisions exist — included here as an OMNI/local seafood spot that is essentially unsuitable for strict celiac or vegan diners.

cuisine: 海鮮丼・食堂 (seafood rice bowls and set meals)
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese seafood diner with no gluten-free accommodation. The kitchen runs on wheat-containing soy sauce and katsuo/kombu dashi (used in simmered fish dishes like aradaki and across set meals). Fried items are central — large shrimp fry, fish fry, and fried oysters — all wheat-battered and almost certainly sharing a single fryer (no dedicated GF fryer). Ise udon, a signature local item, is wheat noodles in a dark wheat-based tare. Sashimi and seafood bowls are the safest raw options, but the dipping soy sauce contains wheat and there is no allergen protocol, so cross-contamination is a real celiac risk. Not recommended for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood specialist where essentially every dish is built on fish, shellfish, or seafood. Set meals and rice bowls feature sashimi, fried shrimp/oysters, squid, abalone and roe. Simmered dishes and Ise udon tare lean on katsuo (bonito) and fish-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu exists; hidden bonito/katsuo dashi should be assumed throughout. Not a viable option for vegans.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-13 JR鳥羽駅名店街
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.842758, 34.486423] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-13. Within target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and well within 0.8km of Toba Station; the spot sits inside the JR Toba Station building / Meitengai arcade, about a 1-minute walk from the station.
seats_text: About 30 seats — tables (6-seat ×2, 2-seat ×3) plus tatami/zashiki seating (6-seat ×2).
hours_raw: 11:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closes earlier when ingredients sell out (ネタが無くなり次第閉店). Closed Tuesdays, and also closed the 2nd and 4th Wednesday of the month.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local station-front diner where cooking is handled essentially by one person — a little spoken Japanese and patience go a long way, and English support should not be expected.
caveats: Dishes are prepared largely by a single cook, so waits can be long at peak times, and the popular seafood bowl often sells out by evening. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation; not suitable for celiacs or vegans.

bio.background: A homey set-meal diner tucked inside the JR Toba Station shopping arcade (Meitengai), just steps from the platforms. It previously operated under the name 大家族 ('big family'), and the warm, family-canteen feel carries through — spacious tatami seating sits alongside ordinary tables, the kind of room built to seat a crowd.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a fishing port town on Ise-Shima Bay, where the day's catch defines the menu.
bio.specialty: The signature is a generous seafood rice bowl heaped with the day's recommended local fish, sashimi-style and spilling over the bowl. The oversized fried shrimp (ebi furai) is a second favorite, and the kitchen also turns out sashimi sets, abalone/sazae grilled in the shell, and local Ise udon.
bio.philosophy: Cook with what the sea gives that day and stop when it's gone — the shop closes once the fish runs out (ネタが無くなり次第閉店), and the prized seafood bowl frequently sells out by evening.

anecdotes.text: Nearly everything is cooked by a single person, so service can be unhurried and waits are part of the experience.
Regulars single out the huge fried shrimp — 'the ebi furai is enormous and a must-order.'
Formerly named 大家族 ('big family'), reflecting its large-group, family-style seating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (shrimp fry, fish fry, fried oysters) are core menu items and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer; assume a single shared fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A Japanese seafood diner relying on standard wheat-based soy sauce for sashimi/donburi and seasoning; no gluten-free soy sauce indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on fish and shellfish with bonito/fish dashi assumed throughout (e.g. simmered aradaki, Ise udon tare); no vegan items.

positives.text: Raw sashimi and seafood rice bowls are the most naturally allergen-light choices, though dipping soy sauce still contains wheat.

neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・JR鳥羽駅 駅ビル内（鳥羽駅 徒歩約1分）

cuisine: さざえ・貝料理（焼貝・海鮮）
gf_detail: A traditional grilled-shellfish stall. Plainly grilled sazae (turban shell), oysters, abalone and other shellfish are naturally gluten-free, and sashimi of abalone/large clam can be eaten without sauce. The risks here are condiments and sides: the shop is known for a "secret tare" basting/dipping sauce that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce, the dipping soy sauce for sashimi contains wheat, and they also serve Ise udon (wheat noodles). No dedicated fryer or allergen handling is documented. A celiac would need to order plain-grilled or raw shellfish with no tare/soy and confirm in Japanese; treat as ask.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated shellfish and seafood grill — turban shells, oysters, abalone, Ise lobster, sea urchin. The entire menu is built on shellfish and seafood, and broths/sauces use seafood. There are no vegan options here.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-7
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.844208, 34.485886] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-7; within target box and ~0.5km of Toba Station, in the さざえストリート row of stalls.
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅周辺（さざえストリート）
seats_text: About 8 seats — a tiny counter-and-table stall in the traditional long-house row of shellfish shops.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily); daytime hours only.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, lively local shellfish stall where staff will put together a plate to your budget — friendly but Japanese-only, so a few key phrases (or pointing) go a long way.
caveats: Daytime-only, cash-first stall in a tightly packed row of similar shellfish shops; confirm exactly which stall you're at. Naturally shellfish-heavy, so not for vegans, and gluten-free diners must steer around the tare sauce, soy sauce and Ise udon.

bio.background: A small, family-style shellfish grill on Toba's "Sazae Street" — the row of red-lantern stalls a few steps from Toba Station where local vendors grill the day's catch right in front of you. Misaki Sazae-ten is one of these long-standing neighborhood spots, an intimate counter-and-table room where you sit close to the grill in a bright, chatty atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled sazae (turban shell) and other shellfish — oysters, abalone, scallops and Ise lobster — plus abalone and large-clam sashimi and sea urchin in season.
bio.philosophy: Tell them your budget and they'll build a recommended spread around it — a relaxed, leave-it-to-us way of eating Toba's seasonal shellfish over a beer or sake.
bio.origin: 鳥羽 (Toba), Mie — Sazae Street near Toba Station

anecdotes.text: Look for the time-worn red lantern that marks the stall; it's a landmark of the Sazae Street row.
If you tell the staff your budget, they prepare a recommended selection to match — handy when you can't decide. Sake and bottled beer are served alongside the shellfish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop is known for a "secret tare" basting/dipping sauce, and sashimi is served with soy sauce — both standard Japanese sauces that contain wheat. They also serve Ise udon (wheat noodles).

vegan_cross_contact.text: A dedicated shellfish/seafood grill (turban shells, oysters, abalone, lobster, sea urchin) — everything is cooked on a shared shellfish grill; no vegan items.

positives.text: Plainly grilled or raw shellfish (sazae, oysters, abalone, clam) ordered without tare or soy sauce is naturally gluten-free; staff will tailor a plate to your request.

cuisine: 喫茶店・自家製ジェラート（伊勢志摩の地元素材）
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅前・鳥羽一番街
gf_detail: A homemade gelato/café shop. Gelato itself is typically wheat-free, but the menu also includes flavors built on traditional ingredients (e.g. kinko-imo dried sweet potato, "Mankin" Japanese herbal-medicine ice) whose components are not detailed publicly, and no gluten-free, dedicated-equipment, or cross-contamination statements are published on the official site or anywhere fetched. There is no dedicated fryer (this is not a fried-food shop). A celiac diner must ask in person about specific flavors, cones, and any cookie/biscuit mix-ins or shared scoops before ordering.
vegan_detail: This is a dairy gelato shop built around fresh raw milk (the official site highlights fresh milk from Oketo, Hokkaido). Signature flavors such as Pearl Latte and Ise Tea are milk-based, and the pearl-powder ("真珠粉") and salt flavors are animal/mineral-derived. Sorbet flavors (e.g. grape sorbet) may exist seasonally but are not confirmed vegan and no dairy-free/vegan labeling is published, so vegan diners should not assume any item is suitable without asking.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目2383-13 鳥羽一番街1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽一丁目2383番地, within the target box and ~150m from Toba Station.
seats_text: A gelato counter/take-away kiosk on the 1F of the Toba Ichibangai building; seating not specified.
closed_days: 不定休（公式サイトのカレンダー参照。掲載情報により木曜定休の記載もあり）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up gelato counter right by Toba Station where a friendly greeting and pointing at flavors gets you far, though staff are unlikely to handle detailed allergy questions in English.

bio.background: Minerva is a homemade-gelato maker named for the Roman goddess of wisdom, with its counter on the first floor of the Toba Ichibangai building right in front of Toba Station. The makers traveled as far as Hokkaido in search of premium milk, building partnerships with producers and pairing that fresh dairy with the bounty of the Ise-Shima coast.
bio.specialty: Pearl Latte (パールラッテ) gelato, made with pearl powder and Ise tea, is the shop's most popular flavor.
bio.philosophy: Delivering it fresh, straight from the factory ('工場から、できたてをお届け!'), and showcasing beloved local Ise-Shima ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The local-flavor lineup leans hard into Ise-Shima terroir: pearl powder, Iwato sea salt, Ise tea, and even kinko-imo, the traditional dried sweet potato that ama divers and fishermen have long eaten as a snack.
The shop's fresh, rich base comes from raw milk sourced from Oketo in Hokkaido, the result of the makers' search for premium dairy.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gelato base is made with fresh dairy milk; no dairy-free or vegan items are labeled, so cross-contact between flavors at a shared scooping counter is likely for any plant-based option.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: the official site lists weekdays/Sat 9:30-17:30 and Sun/holidays 9:00-18:00 with irregular closures, while Tabelog lists Mon-Wed/Fri 09:00-17:00, Sat-Sun/holidays 09:00-18:00, closed Thursdays. Official hours used here. A 2F sister shop, "ミネルヴァ食品館" (a souvenir store), is a separate operation from this 1F gelato counter. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published by the shop; all dietary guidance is inferred from it being a dairy gelato/café and must be confirmed in person.

lead: カモメバヤテラス トバ
name_ja: かもめベイテラス鳥羽
cuisine: カフェ
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅前
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core menu is built around wheat: Ise udon and the signature "Toba SOBA" noodles, cube sandwiches on bread, and fish burgers on buns. Noodle dishes are served with soy-sauce/dashi-based broths and sauces (Ise udon sauce, soba tsuyu) that virtually always contain wheat. No allergen or gluten-free information is published, and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any cross-contamination control. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here; treat as unsuitable.
vegan_detail: No declared vegan menu. The noodle broths and Ise-udon/soba sauces are dashi-based and very likely contain katsuo (bonito); sandwiches are egg- and pork-based; burgers use white moray eel and Ise pork; soft-serve is dairy. The only plausibly plant-forward items are some drinks (matcha latte can be made with non-dairy on request only if available, freshly whisked Ise tea, in-house ground coffee). A strict vegan would need to ask item by item and assume hidden fish stock in anything savory; effectively limited to certain beverages.

seats_text: 44 seats, including eleven 4-top tables.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:00, open daily (no regular closing day listed).
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (営業時間・定休日は変更する場合あり).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual in-station tourist cafe with quick counter-style ordering and takeout focus; comfortable to pop into, but with no published allergen support a GF/vegan visitor should rely on their own guidance rather than expecting staff accommodation.
caveats: Tabelog target string "カモメバヤテラス トバ" is a mis-spelling; verified actual name is かもめベイテラス鳥羽 (former name かもめテラス鳥羽). Opened 2021-07-01 by Kintetsu Retailing inside Toba Station. No vegan or gluten-free information is published anywhere; dietary assessment is inferred from the menu (wheat noodles/bread, dashi broths, dairy, fish) and should be confirmed in person. Not suitable for celiacs.

bio.origin: Opened on July 1, 2021 inside the 2nd-floor concourse of Kintetsu Toba Station as かもめテラス鳥羽 (later かもめベイテラス鳥羽), run by Kintetsu Retailing as a gateway cafe meant to send travelers off with a taste of Ise-Shima.
bio.background: Perched where the tracks meet Toba Bay, the little station cafe was conceived with two jobs: to be a tourism doorway that hands visitors something brimming with local Mie charm, and to be a place that gets people thinking about ocean plastic. It leans hard on regional makers — roasted nori from Toshijima Island, Ise tea from Kinoshita Chaen whisked to order, coffee from Otomoni Coffee in Meiwa ground in-house.
bio.specialty: The signature is "Toba SOBA," a local-noodle creation topped with island-grown seaweed and offered in colour-coded variants (e.g. white with Ise yam, blue with aosa, black with Toshijima roasted nori), alongside Ise udon, quarter-cut cube sandwiches, and bouquet soft-serve including a pearl-salt flavour.
bio.philosophy: Sustainability runs through the whole operation: a declared de-plastic stance using bamboo-bagasse bowls, craft and plant-based PLA cups, paper straws and wooden spoons, with packaging designed to travel spill-free on the train.

anecdotes.text: In autumn 2022 the cafe launched a moray-eel burger (the "TSG Burger") developed by students at Ujiyamada Commercial High School, using increasingly abundant white moray eel from Ise-Shima waters dressed with Ise takuan and Ise-udon sauce; the students said they spent over half a year perfecting the light white-fish patty and its local-specialty sauce.
The shop frames itself as a spot overlooking Ise Bay where customers can pause and consider the plastic-waste problem together, not just grab a snack.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu centers on wheat-based Ise udon, "Toba SOBA" noodles, sandwiches on bread and burgers on buns; no gluten-free items and no published allergen handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodle dishes use soy-sauce/dashi-based broths and sauces (Ise udon sauce, soba tsuyu) which standardly contain wheat; no wheat-free tamari indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Savory items lean on dashi (likely bonito), egg, pork and fish (moray eel burger); soft-serve is dairy — hidden animal stock should be assumed in noodle broths.

positives.text: Drinks are made to order in-house — matcha latte whisked per cup with Ise tea and coffee ground on site — which at least makes single-ingredient beverages a reliable safe fallback.

cuisine: Japanese seafood diner — local Ise-Shima specialties (tekone-zushi, homemade Ise udon, sashimi sets, fried shrimp, seafood bowls)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated and the menu is built around wheat. Ise udon is a thick wheat-flour noodle in a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce contains wheat); tekone-zushi is fish marinated in a soy-based mixture; ebi-fry and tonkatsu use wheat batter/panko and a shared fryer; sashimi and seafood bowls are eaten with wheat-based soy sauce; broths typically rely on soy and bonito. No dedicated fryer or allergen handling mentioned. Not safe for strict gluten-free without direct confirmation, which would be hard to obtain here.
vegan_detail: A seafood-centered diner with no vegan offerings indicated. Core dishes are fish, shellfish (e.g., sazae/turban shell) and shrimp. Japanese dashi and the soy-based sauces for Ise udon and tekone-zushi are typically made with katsuobushi (bonito)/fish stock, so even non-seafood items cannot be assumed plant-based.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-32-6

seats_text: About 30 seats; all non-smoking.
hours_raw: 11:00–20:30; closed Thursdays. Parking available.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local seafood diner where English is unlikely; basic Japanese or a translation app helps, and dietary requests beyond the menu will be hard to convey.

bio.background: A small, long-standing family-run eatery in Toba's 3-chome, billed as the dining spot and lodging closest to the famous Toba Aquarium. An unpretentious local shokujidokoro where tourists and locals stop in for honest, well-priced bowls of the region's seafood.
bio.specialty: Tekone-zushi, the Ise-Shima fisherman's dish of soy-marinated fish (often bonito or tuna) hand-pressed over vinegared rice, served alongside the area's distinctive homemade Ise udon.
bio.philosophy: Showcase the fresh local catch and Ise-Shima regional classics simply and affordably.

anecdotes.text: Promoted by the regional tourism board as the lodging and dining spot closest to Toba Aquarium, pairing a seafood meal with the area's top attraction.
The Ise udon is made in-house, served in the thick, soft style the region is known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (ebi-fry, tonkatsu) use wheat batter/panko; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is indicated, so shared-oil cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes rely on soy sauce / soy-based tare (Ise udon sauce, tekone-zushi marinade) and table soy sauce for sashimi, which in Japan standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on seafood; dashi and soy-based sauces typically contain bonito/fish stock, so even non-seafood items cannot be assumed vegan.

caveats: Identity, location, hours and menu confirmed via Tabelog and the Ise-Shima tourism board. No restaurant-published allergen, GF, or vegan information exists; dietary assessments are inferred from dish types (wheat-heavy, seafood-centered) and should be confirmed directly. One source lists the street number as 3-32-8 vs Tabelog's 3-32-6; geocode used 3-32-6 and lands correctly in the target area.

cuisine: 海鮮焼き・浜焼き（焼き牡蠣、さざえ、あわび、伊勢海老）
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅前サザエストリート
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.844208, 34.485886] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目8-7; within target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and ~0.4km from Toba Station.
seats_text: Small shop, about 15 seats; can fill up and may be full on busy days.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (one source lists hours to 18:00); Thursday closed, with additional irregular closures noted — confirmation recommended.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual local shellfish-grilling counter on a busy seafood lane; expect Japanese-only service and a relaxed point-and-grill style, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised. The core offering is fresh shellfish grilled in the shell (oysters, turban shell, abalone, large clams, scallops) and Ise-lobster, which on their own are naturally gluten-free. However, a diner blog reports sashimi is served "with two kinds of soy sauce" (standard soy sauce contains wheat), and a clam-dashi butter rice is offered as a finisher — dashi-based broths often carry hidden wheat/soy. There is no dedicated fryer or stated cross-contamination control. A celiac should ask for plainly grilled or salt-only shellfish, bring/confirm GF tamari, and avoid soy-sauce dips, dashi rice, and any battered/fried items. Treat as ask/confirm in person.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated grilled-shellfish house — nearly everything centers on oysters, turban shells, abalone, clams, lobster and other seafood. A blog mentions a wakame (seaweed) item, but the shop is built around shellfish and clam/seafood dashi, so there is effectively nothing reliably plant-based. Not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: ザ・貝屋 (The Kaiya) is a specialist grilled-shellfish shop on Toba's famous Sazae Street, the lane of about eleven shellfish-grilling stalls that runs right in front of JR Toba Station. Diners pick what they want straight from the live tanks set inside and out front of the shop, and it is grilled to order on the spot.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — Sazae Street, in front of JR Toba Station.
bio.specialty: Shellfish grilled in the shell — oysters, turban shells (sazae), abalone, large clams (oasari) and Ise spiny lobster — chosen live from the tank. The shop is known for taking care with its oysters in particular, running a sterilization system on its water tanks so it can serve raw oysters safely.
bio.philosophy: Serve the freshest seasonal shellfish possible by letting guests choose from the live tank and grilling it immediately; offer in-season seafood year-round.

anecdotes.text: The shop runs a sterilization unit on its tank water specifically so it can serve good raw oysters, and it is one of the more popular grill houses on Sazae Street — small enough that it can fill up on busy days.

A visiting diner described picking Ise-lobster (served as sashimi with two kinds of soy sauce, then the head grilled), large clams, oysters and hiogi scallops, plus an unusual young-wakame item, finished with a clam-dashi butter rice — all cooked to order right at the counter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi (e.g., Ise-lobster) is served with two kinds of soy sauce; standard soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A dedicated shellfish-grill house; a clam-dashi (seafood broth) butter rice is offered, and the menu centers on oysters, lobster and other shellfish.

positives.text: Shellfish is grilled to order in its own shell after being chosen live from the tank, so plainly grilled or salt-seasoned shellfish can be requested without added sauces.

caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (one lists until 18:00) and the shop has irregular closures beyond its regular Thursday; confirm before visiting. Official site (oyakunitachitai.com) could not be fetched due to a TLS certificate name mismatch, so details rely on the Toba tourism page and diner blogs. No formal allergen or gluten-free policy is published; GF and vegan notes are inferred from menu descriptions and should be confirmed in person.

lead: JUBILEE TEA&BAKES（ジュビリー ティー&ベイクス）
name_ja: ジュビリー ティー&ベイクス
cuisine: 古民家ナチュラルカフェ・英国紅茶（テイラーズ オブ ハロゲイト）と自家製スコーン/ケーキ、ランチのカレー等
gf_detail: This is a British-style tea and bakes cafe: the core offering is wheat-based homemade scones, cakes, muffins and trifles, plus a lunch curry typically thickened with wheat-flour roux. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free baking, or celiac accommodation is mentioned in any source, and there is no indication of a dedicated preparation area or fryer. A flour-dusted bakery kitchen of this kind carries very high airborne and surface cross-contamination risk. Not suitable for celiacs; treat as no GF.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Baked goods (scones, cakes, muffins) and the cream tea set use dairy (clotted cream, butter, likely eggs/milk in batters). The lunch curry's animal-content is undocumented and may contain meat or dairy. Drinks include plain teas (Taylors of Harrogate: Assam, Darjeeling, Earl Grey, English Breakfast, Yorkshire Gold) and fruit cordial sodas which could be ordered black/without milk, but there is no confirmed vegan food item. A vegan diner would need to ask directly; assume limited-to-none for solid food.
