geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽一丁目4番65号 returned [136.842636, 34.483982], within the target box and roughly 0.1km from Toba Station. Note a local business directory lists the address as 鳥羽1丁目5-7 (same block, also within radius); the Tabelog/target address 1-4-65 was used.
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅前（岩崎通り沿い）
seats_text: Small renovated old-house cafe with dine-in seating; exact seat count not published. Note: some reports indicate children under 13 are not accepted for dine-in.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:30 (L.O. 18:00) per directory listings; some sources report 11:30-17:00. Closed Wednesdays plus irregular closures. Current operating days/hours are announced on the official Instagram (@jubileeteaandbakes).
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run Japanese cafe themed around British tea; communication will be mainly in Japanese, so come ready with simple Japanese or a translation app, especially since hours are irregular and best checked on Instagram first.
caveats: Hours are irregular and announced on Instagram (@jubileeteaandbakes); sources disagree (11:00-18:30 vs 11:30-17:00) and closures beyond Wednesday occur, so confirm before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan menu is documented anywhere; this is a wheat- and dairy-forward British bakes cafe and is not suitable for celiacs. Some reports note children under 13 are not accepted for dine-in.

bio.background: JUBILEE TEA&BAKES opened on December 30, 2016, in a renovated old house near Toba Station along Iwasaki Street. It is run by a woman who works the shop largely on her own, in a calm, wood-filled space built around a love of English tea culture.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a tribute to British afternoon-tea tradition transplanted to a small seaside city better known for its aquarium and pearls.
bio.specialty: English tea served by the pot from Taylors of Harrogate (a Royal Warrant holder) — Pure Assam, Afternoon Darjeeling, Earl Grey, English Breakfast and Yorkshire Gold — paired with homemade scones served cream-tea style with imported clotted cream and strawberry jam, alongside cakes, muffins and trifles.
bio.philosophy: A quiet, considered tea experience: proper British leaf tea brewed by the pot and homemade bakes, with attention to sourcing (royal-warrant tea, imported clotted cream, locally roasted coffee from a nearby roastery).

anecdotes.text: The cafe centers on Taylors of Harrogate, a brand that holds a Royal Warrant from the British monarchy, and serves a proper cream tea with two scones, clotted cream and strawberry jam.
Set in a renovated old house near Toba Station, the wood-rich room is built for a calm, unhurried tea; the original-blend coffee is sourced from a nearby roastery.
A local expert feature highlights the shop's royal-warrant Taylors of Harrogate tea and its parfait among Toba destinations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built on wheat: homemade scones, cakes, muffins and trifles plus a lunch curry, all prepared in-house in a flour-using bakery kitchen, creating very high cross-contamination risk for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bakes use dairy and likely eggs (clotted cream, butter, cake batters); no separated vegan preparation is described.

cuisine: 喫茶・洋食食堂（モーニング、ボリューム定食）
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅周辺（鳥羽1丁目）

seats_text: About 58 seats, all non-smoking; counter and table seating, no private rooms.
hours_raw: 07:30-11:00 (L.O. 11:00), 11:00-20:30; closed Wednesdays. Dessert menu unavailable 11:00-14:00.
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running local family diner with a Showa-era feel; expect Japanese-only menu and service, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: This is a classic Western-style diner/coffee shop whose core menu is built on wheat: thick toast, spaghetti, breaded-and-deep-fried shrimp/oyster/chicken (all use flour and panko), and the kitchen shares a single fryer across breaded items, so cross-contamination is essentially guaranteed. Yoshoku sauces and the dashi/seasonings typical of set meals normally contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no published gluten-free menu or allergen information and no indication of celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are indicated. The kitchen is centered on fried meat and seafood, egg- and dairy-rich café items (toast, parfaits, coffee jelly), and set meals that typically rely on fish/bonito dashi and meat. No plant-based menu or substitution is advertised; a vegan would have essentially nothing safe to order here.
caveats: Seat count (about 58) and founding/history details come from a visitor blog and listing aggregators rather than the shop directly; treat as approximate. No official website was found. No allergen or gluten/vegan information is published anywhere, so dietary claims here are inferences from the menu type, not stated policy.

bio.background: A long-standing family-run Western-style diner and coffee shop a few minutes' walk from Toba Station, carrying a warm, nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere with trains visible from the windows. It has built its reputation over the decades on one simple promise: send everyone home full.
bio.specialty: Enormous, generously priced set meals — most famously a fried-chicken set piled over a hidden mountain of spaghetti — alongside crisp jumbo shrimp fry plates, morning thick-toast sets, and hand-brewed coffee.
bio.philosophy: Fill the customer's stomach. Honest, hearty, no-frills home-style cooking in big portions at fair prices.

anecdotes.text: The fried-chicken 'bomb' set comes with roughly sixteen pieces of chicken, and beneath them sits a mound of spaghetti — regulars warn that even hungry growing teenagers will struggle to finish it.
The coffee is poured with real care; the shop's brewing has been associated with national coffee-competition honors, an unexpected touch of craft in a casual diner.
From the seats you can watch the trains roll by, and on lucky days catch a glimpse of the sleek Shimakaze sightseeing express.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded, deep-fried items (shrimp fry, oyster fry, fried chicken) that use flour and panko; a shared fryer makes cross-contamination unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on fried meat and seafood plus egg/dairy café items; shared cooking surfaces and likely fish/bonito dashi in set meals make plant-based dining impractical.

cuisine: 寿司・海鮮（漁師直営）
neighborhood: 鳥羽駅・鳥羽一番街
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1丁目2383-13 鳥羽一番街3F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-2383-13 returned [136.843384, 34.487423], within the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and ~140m from Toba Station. Confirmed in Toba Ichibangai building 3F, directly connected to the station.
seats_text: 11席（座敷4席・テーブル7席）。一部情報では約50席との記載もあり、座席数は資料により異なる。
hours_raw: 11:00〜15:00（売り切れ次第終了）。営業日は土日祝・GW・夏休み等の繁忙期のみ。夏休み期間は連日営業の場合あり。
closed_days: 平日（月〜金）休業。土日祝・繁忙期のみ営業。
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, locally-run fisherman's counter inside a busy station mall where no English or special-diet handling should be expected, so a guide or translation support is the safest way to navigate ingredients here.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The tekone-zushi and chazuke rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and fish/bonito dashi; no allergen handling or dedicated fryer is documented. Not suitable for celiacs without in-person verification.
vegan_detail: Entirely fish-based (sushi, tekone-zushi, seafood chazuke) with bonito/fish dashi throughout. No vegan options.

bio.background: 漁師直営店浜幸 is run by the Hamaguchi family, who were born into a fishing household and operate the 丸栄水産 (Maruei Suisan) fresh-fish business in Shima City. The shop opened in 2006 inside Toba Ichibangai, the mall connected directly to Toba Station, and trades mainly on weekends, holidays, Golden Week and summer high season.
bio.specialty: 数量限定の手こね寿司（てこね寿司）— a local Ise-Shima dish of marinated fish over vinegared rice. The flagship multi-fish version layers honmaguro otoro, chutoro, akami, mebachi tuna, katsuo, kanpachi, sawara, kuromutsu, kue and ikura. The 漁師茶漬け (fisherman's chazuke) is the other house dish.
bio.philosophy: By landing fish through their own Maruei Suisan operation and skipping middlemen, they aim for top freshness at reasonable prices, and they age each fish only as much as it benefits — serving some at peak just-caught freshness and others after aging. The owner says the ultimate measure of success is hearing a child who normally dislikes fish take a bite and call it delicious.
bio.origin: 三重県志摩市・鳥羽（伊勢志摩）

anecdotes.text: 店主は志摩市で鮮魚店『丸栄水産』を営む漁師の家に生まれ、天然魚にしか出せない味わいを大切にしている。
『究極は、魚の苦手なお子様が一口食べた時の「美味しい！」の声です』と店主は語る。
間に業者をはさまず仕入れることで鮮度はもちろん価格もリーズナブルに提供している。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes are soy-sauce-based marinated fish (tekone-zushi) and seafood chazuke; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fully fish-based menu (tuna, katsuo, kanpachi, sawara, ikura, etc.) over vinegared rice; fish and bonito/katsuo dashi are integral, no plant-based dishes identified.

caveats: Operating days are irregular (weekends/holidays and peak seasons only) and the shop closes early once the limited daily quantity sells out, so confirm it is open before visiting. Seat count differs between sources (11 vs ~50). Hours noted as 11:00-15:00 on the official site vs 10:30-15:00 elsewhere; weekend hours modeled as 11:00-15:00.

cuisine: 海鮮・魚介料理（答志島の地魚・刺身・焼貝・干物・あら煮）
neighborhood: 鳥羽（鳥羽一番街3F・鳥羽駅前）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised. This is a casual seafood eatery (shokudo) centered on sashimi, grilled shellfish, dried fish (himono), whitebait (jako), and simmered fish (ara-ni). Sashimi is served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat and is not safe for celiacs. Cooked dishes such as ara-ni typically use shoyu/mirin-based broth (wheat risk), and any fried preparations carry battered-coffee/tempura wheat and shared-oil cross-contamination risk. No allergen information, ingredient sourcing, or dedicated-fryer information is published. A celiac cannot rely on this kitchen without direct confirmation, and even then the soy-sauce and shared-equipment risks remain unaddressed. Treat as not GF-safe.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-specialist restaurant; essentially every dish is fish or shellfish based. Any rice/soup accompaniments would almost certainly use katsuo (bonito) or fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu is offered or advertised. Not a viable option for vegans.

bio.background: A casual seafood eatery on the 3rd floor of Toba Ichiban-gai, the shopping arcade attached to Toba Station on Toba Bay. The kitchen sources its fish and shellfish from Toshijima (Tashijima), the fishing-and-tourism island that floats in Toba Bay.
bio.specialty: Fresh Toshijima seafood served as sashimi, grilled shellfish (yakigai), whitebait (jako), dried fish (himono), and simmered fish-head stew (ara-ni); a popular kaisen-don bowl tops rice with eight items including sea bream, yellowtail, tuna, squid, whitebait, seaweed, sweet shrimp, and salmon roe.
bio.philosophy: Deliver the day's catch from Toshijima in Toba Bay directly to the table.

anecdotes.text: The shop's tagline celebrates the island catch line by line: sashimi, grilled shellfish, whitebait, dried fish, ara-ni.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and seafood bowls are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat and unsafe for celiacs; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

bio.background: A casual seafood teahouse on the third floor of the Toba Ichiban-gai complex, directly linked to Toba Station, serving the catch of the Ise-Shima coast to travelers stepping off the train. The shop has been running for roughly a decade, and at its tenth anniversary it rolled out a celebration menu pulling together its most-loved plates.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the 焼きたこ膳 (grilled octopus set) made from locally landed octopus — an original dish you won't find elsewhere, praised by visitors for octopus that is tender yet full of flavor, served alongside fresh sashimi. The kitchen also leans into Shima's hometown 郷土料理: てこね寿司 (soy-marinated fish tossed through vinegared rice), plus sea urchin-and-sashimi sets, seafood chirashi, negitoro bowls, and a shio-sai bowl of shrimp, octopus and squid.
bio.philosophy: The house keeps things 手ごろ — honest, affordable prices on regional seafood so that the local catch stays within reach of ordinary travelers rather than being a special-occasion splurge.

anecdotes.text: Marked its 10th year in business by launching a special menu gathering its most popular dishes together.
The grilled octopus, made from local fresh octopus, is described by visitors as tender with a deep octopus flavor, and is unique to this shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried items (squid fries in the shio-sai bowl) and grilled seafood sets; no dedicated fryer or allergen separation is documented, so wheat cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Core dishes — sashimi, kaisen-don, negitoro-don and the local てこね寿司 — rely on soy sauce / soy-based marinades, which in Japan are typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a seafood kitchen; rice and soup items would be prepared with fish-based dashi and there are no vegan dishes.

bio.background: A long-standing seafood dining spot near Toba Station that has operated for around 28 years, drawing visitors from beyond the prefecture to taste the catch of nearby Ise Bay.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a port town on Ise Bay known for its fishing and pearl culture.
bio.specialty: The seafood rice bowl (kaisen-don, ~¥2,650) layered with about nine kinds of fresh seafood, an original sauce, and premium salted salmon tucked into the rice; the natural tiger-prawn fried set and the assorted seasonal sashimi platter are also house favorites.
bio.philosophy: A simple, honest aim: to let guests enjoy fresh, quality ingredients pulled mainly from Ise Bay, served in a relaxed local setting.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant says it has welcomed guests for roughly 28 years and draws diners from other prefectures who come for the Ise Bay seafood.
Their signature kaisen-don hides premium salted salmon inside the rice beneath about nine kinds of seafood and an original sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Multiple fried sets (shrimp, white fish, mixed fry) use wheat batter/breading and share fryer oil; no dedicated gluten-free fryer.

bio.background: A small, independent seafood eatery tucked into the Toba station-front shopping arcade (駅前商店街), a one-minute walk from Toba Station. The kitchen works with fish and shellfish delivered straight from local fishermen, leaning on the bounty of Ise Bay.
bio.specialty: Ise Bay shellfish at its simplest and best — Ise spiny lobster (伊勢えび), abalone (あわび), turban shell (さざえ), large clams (大あさり), hard clams (はまぐり), and grilled oysters (焼きカキ) — alongside an unusually deep selection of Mie regional sake, including bottles like the sought-after 'Jikon (而今)'.
bio.philosophy: Let the day's catch speak for itself: direct-from-the-fisherman seafood, grilled or served fresh, paired with local sake.

anecdotes.text: The spot is noted for carrying one of the widest ranges of Mie regional sake of any restaurant near Toba Station, including the rare and coveted 'Jikon (而今)'.
Counter seating makes it an easy stop for a solo diner.

positives.text: Core offerings are grilled and raw Ise Bay shellfish (lobster, abalone, sazae, clams, oysters) that are naturally wheat-free in their unseasoned form.

bio.origin: Toba, Mie — on the seafront cluster of shellfish stalls known as Sazae Street, a one-minute walk from Toba Station.
bio.background: A long-established shellfish specialist said to have been founded around 1956 (昭和31年), part of the row of small grill shops that line Toba's Sazae Street.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled turban shell (さざえの壺焼き) eaten on the spot, alongside large clams (大あさり), hiōgi scallops, and oysters pulled straight from the tank; small-batch uni rice and aosa-seaweed miso soup also appear.
bio.philosophy: Keep it simple and fresh — shellfish taken from the tank and grilled to order, served at a tiny counter to be eaten right there or taken away.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for its energetic proprietress (元気な女将さん) and has built such a devoted following that some travelers come to Toba specifically to eat here.
Shellfish is taken live from the tank and cooked on the spot; turban shell, large clams, oysters and hiōgi scallops are the favorites, with the option to eat in or buy as a souvenir.

positives.text: The menu centers on plain charcoal-grilled shellfish (turban shell, large clam, scallop, oyster), which is naturally wheat-free when prepared without soy sauce or batter.

cuisine: Seafood and shellfish hamayaki (charcoal-grilled shellfish you pick yourself), plus grilled oysters, sazae (turban shell) tsuboyaki, abalone, spiny lobster, and sashimi. A casual snack-and-souvenir spot on Toba's "Sazae Street."
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅前（さざえストリート）

However there is no published allergen handling and no dedicated fryer information. The danger points are wheat-containing soy sauce used in/over sazae tsuboyaki (turban-shell broth often seasoned with shoyu) and any dipping soy sauce, plus possible shared grill/utensil surfaces. A celiac should request items grilled plain with no soy sauce / no shared brush, and ask before eating tsuboyaki. Treat as "ask on site."

bio.background: A small shellfish-grill and souvenir shop on Toba's lively "Sazae Street," just a couple of minutes' walk from Toba Station's south exit. The counter is lined with fresh shells from Ise-Shima waters, and the appeal is simple: you choose the shellfish you like and the staff grill it for you right there.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled shellfish hamayaki — especially sazae (turban shell) tsuboyaki, grilled oysters made in-house, abalone, and spiny lobster — eaten standing-style as a snack or bought as a souvenir.
bio.philosophy: Let visitors taste the bounty of the Ise-Shima sea on the spot, fresh and unfussy.

anecdotes.text: Customers pick whichever shells they fancy from the display and the staff grill them on the spot — a hands-on, point-and-grill experience that makes it a popular quick stop on Sazae Street.
Listed by the Toba tourism association as a Sazae Street grilled-turban-shell spot offering casual bites plus spiny lobster, abalone and shellfish to take home as souvenirs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No published allergen or cross-contamination policy; shellfish are grilled on shared charcoal grills and a celiac should ask for items grilled plain without soy sauce or a shared basting brush.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sazae tsuboyaki (turban shell) and dipping sauces are commonly seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; confirm before eating and ask for no-shoyu preparation if you need strict GF.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a shellfish/seafood operation with seafood-based grills and broths; not suitable for vegans and cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

positives.text: Plainly charcoal-grilled (素焼き) shellfish and sashimi are naturally gluten-free when served without soy sauce, giving a celiac workable options if requested.

gf_detail: 焼き貝はその場で炭火焼きにされ、味付けは塩のほか「醤油ベース」の調味が用いられると複数の食レポで記載。一般的な醤油には小麦が含まれるため、セリアックの方には不向き。専用フライヤーや調理器具の分離、クロスコンタミ防止策に関する公開情報はなく、店側のアレルギー対応も確認できない。素材自体（さざえ・大あさり・牡蠣等の貝類）はグルテンフリーだが、味付け・刺し身用の付け醤油・共用の焼き場での扱いに注意が必要。来店時に「塩のみ・醤油なし」での提供が可能か直接確認すること。
vegan_detail: 貝専門の浜焼き店で、メニューはさざえ・大あさり・牡蠣・はまぐり等の貝類および伊勢海老の刺し身が中心。植物性のみの料理は提供されておらず、ヴィーガン対応は不可。

bio.background: 鳥羽駅前にのびる「サザエストリート」に10軒ほど並ぶ焼き貝店のひとつ。海から直接海水を引き込んだ生簀を備え、その水槽の中ではさざえや大あさりに加えて伊勢海老までが生きたまま泳いでいる。獲れたての地元の貝を、その場で炭火に乗せて豪快に焼き上げる、昔ながらの素朴な浜焼きの店。
bio.specialty: 焼貝の盛り合わせ（牡蠣・さざえ・はまぐり・大あさり等を一皿に。約1,000円前後）。注文に応じて大きな伊勢海老を刺し身にしてくれることもある。
bio.philosophy: 海水生簀による圧倒的な鮮度と、目の前で焼く臨場感。貝に不慣れな人でも色々な種類を試して自分の好みを見つけられるよう、気取らず楽しめる一皿を目指している。
roles: 元海女のベテランスタッフが焼き手を務める

anecdotes.text: 水槽の海水は海から直接引き込まれており、身がパンパンに膨らんだ鮮度の良い貝が並ぶ。
ベテランの（元海女の）スタッフが絶妙な焼き加減で貝を仕上げてくれると評判。
大あさり・さざえ・はまぐりなどを、素材ごとに塩や醤油ベースなど味付けを変えて焼き上げる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 共用の炭火焼き場で全ての貝を焼くため、醤油を使う品との調理面での分離は期待しにくい。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 貝・伊勢海老など海産物専門で、植物性のみの料理は提供されていない。

positives.text: 素材は貝類が中心で、塩のみで焼けばグルテンを避けられる可能性がある（要相談）。

cuisine: 海女小屋スタイルの海鮮浜焼き。サザエ・大あさり・ヒオウギ貝・牡蠣・伊勢海老など新鮮な貝・魚介の炭火焼き中心
gf_detail: This is a charcoal hama-yaki (grilled shellfish) shop. Plainly charcoal-grilled shellfish (sazae, oysters, clams, hiougi scallops, ise-ebi) are naturally gluten-free at the ingredient level, which is the safest path here. However, no celiac-specific protocols are documented: soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) is commonly offered as a tabletop/finishing condiment, and any dipping sauces or non-grilled items may contain wheat. There is no information on cross-contamination control or a dedicated fryer (the shop centers on grilling, not frying). A celiac should ask for items grilled plain with no soy sauce and confirm no shared sauces touch the food. Communicate the wheat allergy clearly before ordering.
vegan_detail: An ama-hut style seafood grill built entirely around live shellfish and fish (turban shell, oysters, clams, scallops, spiny lobster) plus sashimi. There are no documented vegan dishes, and even side seasonings/dashi would derive from seafood. Not a viable option for vegan diners.

bio.background: A small ama-goya (diver's hut) style seafood grill tucked into Toba's 'Sazae Street' shopping lane, just steps from the south side of Toba Station. The shop is run in the spirit of the local ama tradition, the free-diving women of the Toba and Shima coast who have harvested shellfish from Ise Bay for generations.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie Prefecture, on the Ise Bay coast
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled shellfish (hama-yaki): turban shell (sazae) tsubo-yaki, large clams, hiougi scallops, Matoya Bay rock oysters, and spiny lobster (ise-ebi), alongside sashimi.
bio.philosophy: Fresh local Ise Bay shellfish, grilled simply over charcoal and eaten hot, in a relaxed, conversational counter setting.

anecdotes.text: A former ama (diving woman) does the grilling herself, and regulars say her timing on the shellfish is 'exquisite,' while she happily talks with guests about the diving life and the best way to eat each shellfish in season.
Visitors describe the small counter filling with the aroma of grilling shellfish, working through sazae tsubo-yaki, plump Matoya Bay rock oysters, hiougi scallops, and a whole grilled ise-ebi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is commonly offered as a finishing condiment at hama-yaki shellfish grills; standard soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce is documented here, so a celiac should request shellfish grilled plain without soy sauce.

positives.text: The core offering is shellfish and lobster grilled plainly over charcoal, which at the ingredient level is naturally free of gluten and easy to order without added sauces.

caveats: Hours vary between sources (Tabelog 10:00-17:00 vs. tourism listing 9:00-17:30) and are seasonal/irregular; confirm before visiting. Reservations are generally not accepted, though a phone call may sometimes secure a slot. Dietary details (soy sauce, sauces, cross-contamination) are inferred from the hama-yaki format and a visitor blog, not from any restaurant allergy statement — verify in person.

gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a Japanese seafood/kaiseki and buffet restaurant where soy sauce (wheat-based shoyu) and bonito/kombu dashi are central to nearly every dish; tempura and grilled items are likely. The official site provides an allergy-inquiry form but explicitly states that cross-contact with allergens is possible because shared kitchens and equipment serve multiple menus, and that guests must self-assess whether dining is safe. There is no dedicated fryer or gluten-free preparation. Not suitable for celiacs; at best you must inquire in advance via the allergy form and accept the venue's own caveat that cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is advertised. The cuisine is built around local seafood (spiny lobster, abalone) and traditional Japanese cooking that relies on katsuo (bonito) and other fish-based dashi, so hidden animal stock is pervasive even in vegetable dishes and the breakfast buffet. The restaurant does feature hydroponically grown vegetables, but no plant-based set or dashi-free preparation is described. A vegan would need to use the advance allergy/dietary inquiry form and confirm individually; nothing here is reliably vegan off the shelf.

caveats: This is the evening dining restaurant of the Toda-ya ryokan (not a standalone walk-in eatery); it operates on a complete-advance-reservation basis and is most accessible to hotel guests. bio.background: Kochi to Umi is the rotating observatory restaurant of the long-standing Toda-ya ryokan in Toba, opened on July 21, 2017 on the top of the inn's South Building. Billed as the only rotating observation restaurant in the Tokai region, the floor slowly turns so that diners take in a full sweep of Toba Bay and the Ise-Shima coastline as the sun sets over the water.
bio.specialty: Tableside-finished kaiseki featuring Ise-Shima seafood — local spiny lobster (Ise-ebi) and abalone — prepared in front of guests, alongside seasonal Japanese-Western dishes.
bio.philosophy: A 'banquet of taste and view': pairing the slow-turning panorama of Toba Bay with heartfelt, course-by-course cooking of local ingredients, including hydroponically grown vegetables introduced as fresh, health-conscious choices. Staff present each dish in turn and pace the meal to the guest.

anecdotes.text: The dining room slowly rotates so guests see a complete panorama of Toba Bay and Ise-Shima while they eat, marketed as the only rotating observation restaurant in the Tokai region.
Local Ise-Shima ingredients such as spiny lobster and abalone are finished tableside (客前会席), and a Japanese-Western breakfast buffet of around 50 items is offered.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The official site states cross-contact with allergens is possible because shared kitchens and equipment serve multiple menus, and asks guests to self-assess whether they can dine safely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese seafood and kaiseki cooking here relies on standard soy sauce (wheat-based) and fish dashi; no wheat-free shoyu or gluten-free preparation is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cuisine centers on local seafood and likely bonito/fish dashi even in non-fish dishes; no dashi-free or plant-based set is described, so animal-derived stock is expected throughout including the buffet.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel provides an allergy-inquiry form for guests to submit dietary needs in advance, but pairs it with the caveat that cross-contact cannot be ruled out.

positives.text: An advance allergy-inquiry form is offered, and the kitchen highlights fresh hydroponically grown vegetables, giving a starting point for arranging modified dishes ahead of time.

roles: 老舗旅館の館内喫茶コーナー

gf_detail: A crepe and galette takeout stand — the core product is wheat-flour batter, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners. There is no published information indicating any gluten-free batter or dedicated preparation, no separate equipment, and no allergen statement. Crepes are cooked on a shared griddle, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable. Toppings and fillings can be adjusted, but the crepe base itself contains gluten. Treat as not suitable for gluten-free; if you must inquire, ask directly about ingredients, but assume wheat throughout.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. Standard crepe batter normally contains egg and milk, and dessert crepes typically use whipped cream, custard, ice cream, chocolate and butter — so most items are unlikely to be vegan as-prepared. The shop emphasizes that fillings can be customized, which may allow some plant-based topping combinations, but the batter itself almost certainly is not vegan. There is no confirmation of a plant-based batter or dairy-free cream. A vegan diner would need to ask the staff in detail about the batter (egg/milk) and each topping before ordering, and should not assume any item is safe without confirmation.

bio.background: Ephemere is a takeout crepe stand opened on December 20, 2025 by three students of Toba National College of Maritime Technology's advanced engineering course: Taketo Nishii, Kensuke Nakamura, and Yushi Hamaguchi, all in their early twenties. The trio shared a desire to start their own business, and the company operating the Toba Ichibankai shopping building responded by offering them a vacant second-floor storefront right beside Toba Station.
bio.specialty: Takeout crepes, inspired by the French Breton galette tradition, with fillings that can be customized to the customer's taste.
bio.philosophy: The name 'Ephemere' is French for 'fleeting' or 'short-lived' — a fitting nod to a venture that is deliberately limited in time: the lease runs only through February 2027, around the founders' graduation. It captures the spirit of a here-and-now project the students pour themselves into while still in school.

anecdotes.text: Three Toba Maritime College students who simply wanted to try running a business were handed a real storefront when the building's operator offered them an empty second-floor space — turning a student ambition into an actual shop right at the station.
The shop is intentionally temporary: its lease and its life run through February 2027, matching the founders' graduation — the 'ephemeral' name is literal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Crepes are cooked on a shared griddle/hot plate; the batter is wheat flour, so the product is inherently not gluten-free and cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

positives.text: Fillings/toppings can be customized to the customer's preference, which gives some flexibility in choosing what goes on the crepe (though not the wheat batter itself).

caveats: Newly opened (Dec 2025), limited-time venture through Feb 2027 run by three students; phone number is not published and no menu/allergen details are available online, so all dietary specifics must be confirmed in person. PayPay and cash accepted.

bio.background: Awako sits on the second floor of Pearl Plaza inside Mikimoto Pearl Island, the small island in Toba Bay where Kokichi Mikimoto succeeded in cultivating the world's first pearl in 1893. The restaurant takes its name from 'Awako,' the udon shop that was Mikimoto's family home, tying the meal directly to the founder's roots.
bio.specialty: A 'pearl set' centered on Ise udon paired with dishes made from akoya pearl-oyster scallop (貝柱) — including scallop rice, soy-braised scallop, fried scallop, and uni-dressed scallop — with a quail egg playing the part of a 'pearl' on the plate.
bio.philosophy: Carry the founder's family-udon heritage forward and let visitors taste the akoya oyster itself, serving its scallop year-round rather than only in winter when it normally appears in markets.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is named after the udon shop that was founder Kokichi Mikimoto's family home, linking the menu's Ise udon back to his origins.
It serves akoya pearl-oyster scallop year-round — a rare ingredient that normally only reaches markets in winter.
A diner described the pearl set's Ise udon as soft and fluffy in a sweet soy-based broth, with the uni-dressed scallop as the standout, eaten beside windows overlooking Toba Bay.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried/battered akoya scallop, typically wheat batter cooked in shared oil; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature Ise udon is served in a sweet soy-sauce-based tare; soy-braised and other scallop dishes also use soy sauce, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood-centered menu (akoya scallop, uni) with Ise udon broth that commonly uses bonito dashi; no vegan options advertised.

bio.background: Kiyoishi is the teppanyaki restaurant of Toba International Hotel Shiojitei, set on the coast of Toba in Mie Prefecture. The chefs personally visit the market at Kashikojima/Kashii area to source ingredients, often serving them the same day they are bought, so that much of the menu reflects what was freshest that morning.
bio.specialty: Teppanyaki courses built around Ise lobster (伊勢海老), abalone (鮑), and premium wagyu such as Matsusaka beef, grilled to order at the counter in front of guests.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows a 旬産旬消 ('eat the season in its season') philosophy — celebrating the bounty of the Ise-Shima coast, including lobster and abalone of the kind historically offered at the Ise Grand Shrine, by serving local ingredients at their seasonal peak.

anecdotes.text: The chefs go to the local market themselves each day to choose ingredients, and many dishes are prepared the very day the seafood is purchased — so the course can change with what the sea gives up that morning.
The lobster and abalone served here are the same prized Ise-Shima delicacies long associated with offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine, tying the meal to the region's culinary heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Teppanyaki is cooked on a single shared iron griddle alongside wheat-containing seasonings and other guests' dishes, creating real cross-contamination risk; no gluten-free protocol is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built on seafood and beef cooked on a shared griddle with seafood- and meat-based fats and sauces; no plant-only preparation is described.

cuisine: Hotel café-lounge: house-made cakes, seasonal afternoon tea, light lunch and a bar, with panoramic Toba Bay views
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published by the venue. As a Western-style hotel café, the core offering is built around wheat: cakes, pastries, scones, croque monsieur, burgers and afternoon-tea tiers all contain flour. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no cross-contamination protocol is stated anywhere. A celiac diner cannot assume any item is safe — you would need to call ahead (0599-25-3121) and confirm directly with the hotel before relying on anything. Treat as ask-only and high-risk for cross-contact in a shared bakery/pastry kitchen.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. The menu centers on dairy- and egg-rich patisserie (cheesecake, cakes, scones with clotted cream, afternoon-tea sweets) plus savory items like burgers; these are not vegan as standard. Light meals and lunch may contain hidden katsuo/bonito dashi typical of Japanese kitchens. There is no listed plant-based set. A vegan would need to contact the hotel in advance to ask whether anything can be prepared; do not assume any current item is vegan.

neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目 (Toba 1-chome), at Toba Kokusai Hotel, about an 11-minute walk north of Toba Station's north exit
seats_text: 51 seats total (46 table seats plus 5 bar counter seats)
hours_raw: Open daily. Café/lounge 10:00–17:00 and bar 18:00–22:00 per the official site (Tabelog lists a continuous 10:00–22:30). Cakes 10:00–17:00 (LO 16:30). Lunch 11:30–14:00 (LO 13:30). Afternoon tea seatings around 11:30 and 13:30 (90 min), reservation required by 5pm the previous day. Light meals & takeout 11:30–17:00 (LO 16:30). Bar 18:00–22:00 (LO 21:30).
cultural_comfort_note: As a lounge inside an established international hotel that welcomes outside visitors, it is one of the more approachable, English-comfortable stops in the area.
caveats: No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published by the venue; all dietary safety notes here are inferred from the menu type and must be confirmed by phone before a celiac or strict-vegan diner relies on anything. Hours differ between the official site (10–17 café, 18–22 bar) and Tabelog (10:00–22:30); the hours_week reflects the broad daily envelope. Afternoon tea requires advance reservation by 5pm the previous day even though general seating is walk-in.

bio.background: The Lobby Lounge sits on the Ocean Wing entrance floor of Toba Kokusai Hotel, a long-established resort hotel perched above Toba Bay. Wrapped in expansive windows, it is built around the view of the bay and its islands, and it reopened after a renovation in spring 2024.
bio.specialty: House-made cakes and a seasonal, themed afternoon tea served on a three-tier stand, alongside coffee enjoyed against the Toba Bay panorama.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed seaside lounge open to both hotel guests and outside visitors, pairing hotel-made sweets and light meals with the scenery of the bay.

anecdotes.text: The lounge reopened in spring 2024 after a renovation, refreshing the space that overlooks Toba Bay.
Seasonal afternoon tea rotates with themed collaborations, served as a three-tier stand of savory bites and house-made sweets and scones, by reservation.

name_ja: 鳥羽 海鮮料理 屋形船 おきた
cuisine: 海鮮料理・屋形船クルージング（伊勢海老・鮑・牡蠣の浜焼き）
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅前
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-2383-51
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.843384, 34.487423] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-2383-51; within target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and within ~0.8km of Toba Station.
seats_text: 約48席（屋形船の船内、掘りごたつ式の座席）
hours_raw: 12:00-21:30（年中無休）。昼の出航12:30、夜の出航18:30。出航15分前までに集合。
cultural_comfort_note: This is a reservation-only Japanese houseboat cruise experience with set seafood courses and scheduled departures, so come with a guide or strong Japanese support; dietary requests must be arranged in advance via the booking remarks and there is no indication English or allergy accommodation is available.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated anywhere. Set seafood courses (grilled/steamed lobster, abalone, oysters, shellfish浜焼き) are seasoned and accompanied by typical Japanese sauces; standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) and dashi are virtually certain to be present in seasonings, dipping sauces, and prepared dishes. No menu detail confirms wheat-free preparation, no dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls are mentioned, and there is no allergen program. A celiac diner cannot consider this safe without confirming every component directly with the kitchen in advance.
vegan_detail: Not viable. The entire concept is fresh seafood (spiny lobster, abalone, oysters, scallops, turban shells, clams). Courses are seafood-built and would rely on fish/katsuo dashi and shellfish; there is no vegan or vegetarian course or option mentioned. A vegan could not be served a meaningful meal here.
caveats: Houseboat (yakatabune) experience: reservations required (typically book in advance; lobster course requires booking by 8:00 AM same day, min 2 people). Scheduled departures (lunch ~12:30, dinner ~18:30) — arrive ~15 minutes early. Menu changes with daily catch. Paid parking nearby (~¥500/2hrs).

bio.background: Run by Marine Okita Co., a Toba-based company with roughly 60 years of experience serving meals on boats. In 2014 the company launched this purpose-built yakatabune (traditional Japanese houseboat restaurant), described as the only formal yakatabune in Mie Prefecture, with a rooftop observation deck offering open panoramic views over Toba Bay.
bio.specialty: Local Ise spiny lobster (伊勢海老), abalone (鮑), and oysters (牡蠣) served as grilled seafood (浜焼き) on the boat — signature courses pair a half lobster grilled with mixed shellfish and a half steamed with vegetables (屋形蒸し).
bio.philosophy: To offer a special, out-of-the-everyday space where guests can safely enjoy local seafood while taking in the scenery of Toba Bay, sourcing ingredients fresh and locally.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen sources seafood fresh from the market each day, so the courses shift with the daily catch.
Billed as Mie Prefecture's only formal yakatabune, the boat carries a rooftop observation deck for panoramic day and night views over Toba Bay.
Marine Okita Co. drew on around 60 years of serving meals aboard boats before opening this custom-built houseboat restaurant in 2014.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The online booking form includes a remarks/notes section where customers can write health concerns for staff to respond to, but no allergen program or gluten-free/vegan accommodation is advertised.

cuisine: 料理旅館・日帰りバイキング（伊勢志摩の海の幸、石窯ピザ）
neighborhood: 鳥羽温泉郷・鳥羽湾沿い
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. This is a large Japanese seafood/Western/Chinese buffet (50+ items) with a stone-oven pizza station and a make-fresh "live kitchen," so wheat is heavily present: pizza dough, tempura/fried items, soy-sauce-based seasonings, and likely wheat in shoyu and dashi-based broths. The buffet marks allergens with colored seals covering eggs, crustaceans, WHEAT (小麦), dairy, and sesame/nuts on individual dishes — but the kitchen explicitly states that allergens in condiments and broths may NOT be indicated, and unlisted concerns should be raised with staff in advance. A shared buffet line with breaded/fried items and pizza nearby makes cross-contamination very likely; no dedicated fryer is indicated. A celiac should contact the inn ahead of time, not rely on the open buffet.
vegan_detail: No vegan program. As a seafood-forward Ise-Shima ryokan buffet, most dishes rely on fish, katsuo/bonito dashi, and other animal products, and broths/seasonings are not fully allergen-labeled. A salad bar, vegetables, fruit, rice, and some Western/Chinese items exist so a vegetarian could assemble a plate, but hidden dashi/katsuo in cooked dishes is a real risk and there is no marked plant-based menu. Vegans should arrange needs directly with the inn in advance.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-24-26
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.84137, 34.487953] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-24-26 — within the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and roughly 250m from Toba Station, consistent with the stated ~3 min walk.
seats_text: Large ryokan with roughly 430 dining capacity, including a buffet dining hall (天・地・海) and banquet/restaurant seating.
hours_raw: Day-trip dinner buffet (天・地・海 / しんわダイニング) approx. 18:00-19:30 last entry; lunch kaiseki 11:30-13:00 by reservation. Open year-round except maintenance days.
closed_days: 年中無休（メンテナンス日を除く）
cultural_comfort_note: A large, well-established Japanese ryokan used to welcoming guests; staff are professional and used to dietary questions, but expect to communicate dietary needs in Japanese (or arrange in advance) since the day-trip buffet is mainly a domestic-tourist setting.

bio.background: Todaya is a long-established culinary inn (料理旅館) on the shore of Toba Bay, founded in 1830 (Tenpō 1). It grew up along the old Sangū pilgrimage road that carried travelers from across Japan to the Ise Shrines, and it has welcomed those pilgrims and visitors for nearly two centuries.
bio.origin: 鳥羽 (Toba), Ise-Shima, Mie — on the pilgrimage route to Ise.
bio.specialty: A lavish Ise-Shima seafood buffet served at the open 'live kitchen' dining hall 天・地・海 (Tenchikai), where fresh local sashimi and sushi share the floor with a dramatic whole-fish filleting show and a stone oven turning out hand-stretched pizza topped with seasonal ingredients.
bio.philosophy: A focus on omotenashi — hospitality expressed through care for ingredients, cooking, atmosphere, and service — rooted in its history of looking after pilgrims and guests.

anecdotes.text: The dinner buffet features a theatrical jizakana kaitai show, where chefs break down a whole local fish in front of guests before it is served as sashimi.
Stone-oven pizza is made on site from handmade dough topped with seasonal produce, served alongside Japanese, Western, and Chinese dishes from a roughly 10-meter open kitchen counter.
Founded in 1830, the inn sits along the former Sangū pilgrimage road and has served Ise-bound travelers for generations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Open all-you-can-eat buffet with a stone-oven pizza station, fried/breaded items, and a shared serving line, making cross-contact with wheat very likely; no dedicated fryer indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen states that allergens contained in condiments and broths (e.g., soy sauce, dashi) may not be reflected on the per-dish allergen labels.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-forward buffet; fish, bonito/katsuo dashi, and animal products are pervasive and shared serving utensils are used across dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Individual buffet dishes are marked with colored allergen seals covering eggs, crustaceans, wheat, dairy, and sesame/nuts; guests with allergies not shown are asked to consult staff.
The inn lists 'allergy display available' (アレルギー表示あり) among its dining services.

positives.text: Per-dish allergen labeling is provided at the buffet for major allergens including wheat, which lets diners screen many items at a glance.
A salad bar, fresh vegetables, fruit, and freshly cooked local rice give some naturally lower-risk options.

caveats: This is primarily an overnight ryokan; the relevant offering for non-guests is the day-trip (日帰り) dinner buffet, which is completely reservation-based. As a large seafood buffet with pizza and fried items it is not a strong celiac-safe or vegan venue — anyone with strict gluten or plant-based needs should arrange requirements with the inn well in advance rather than rely on the open buffet line. Exact day-trip buffet hours vary by plan/season; confirm at booking.

cuisine: ラーメン（焼肉店併設の〆ラーメン・松阪牛ホルモンラーメン）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a ramen shop: noodles are wheat, and the signature bowls include a rich soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen and a Matsusaka-beef hormone ramen, all built on wheat-containing soy sauce and meat-based broths. Fried gyoza and chicken wings (手羽先) are also wheat-coated/fried in shared oil. No dedicated fryer, no dedicated GF preparation, and nothing on any source page indicating celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diner.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The shop is the late-night ramen annex of a yakiniku/hormone (offal) restaurant; its signature bowls are meat-based (Matsusaka-beef hormone ramen) and ramen broths and soy-sauce tare here would contain animal and/or fish stock. No plant-based options are advertised on any source. Side dishes (gyoza, chicken wings) are also animal products.

seats_text: Small cozy space — counter seating plus a couple of tables, about 12 seats total.
hours_raw: 17:00–24:00 (Mon–Sat & holidays). Closed Sundays. If a Monday falls on a public holiday, the shop closes the following Tuesday instead. Hours may change during consecutive-holiday periods.
closed_days: Sundays (irregular substitution: if a Monday is a public holiday, closes Tuesday instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny local late-night ramen counter aimed at Japanese regulars and yakiniku diners coming for a finishing bowl; no English signage or menu indicated, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude help.
caveats: Closed-day differs across listings: Tabelog and HotPepper say closed Sundays (open Mon–Sat), while the Ise-Shima tourism page lists closed Mondays — confirm by phone (0599-37-7069). Cash only.

bio.background: A hidden little ramen counter tucked behind the popular Taishu Horumon Yakiniku Sou (大衆ホルモン焼肉創) near Toba City Hall. It opened on December 31, 2019, built around a simple, fun idea: after filling up on grilled meat and offal at the yakiniku shop out front, you slip around back for a finishing bowl of ramen and a drink.
bio.specialty: Rich coarse-filtered shoyu ramen (粗濾濃厚醤油ラーメン) and a Matsusaka-beef hormone ramen (松阪牛ホルモンラーメン); chicken wings (手羽先) and fried gyoza are popular sides.
bio.philosophy: An after-hours 'shime' (finishing-bowl) spot — keep it small, late, and playful rather than a destination ramen-ya.

anecdotes.text: The shop has a playful house rule: win an arm-wrestling match and get a discount on your bill.
It runs as the back-room ramen annex of the yakiniku shop Sou, so diners eat grilled meat out front and then come around back for ramen and drinks.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard ramen kitchen: wheat noodles, wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and shared fryer/oil for gyoza and chicken wings mean heavy gluten cross-contact throughout. No GF handling indicated on any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls include a rich shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, and Japanese soy sauce is standardly brewed with wheat. Assume wheat present in the tare/broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat-forward menu (Matsusaka-beef hormone ramen) at the back of a yakiniku/offal restaurant; broths and tare are animal/fish based. No plant-based options found.

lead: 鳥羽国際ホテル もんど岬
name_ja: 鳥羽国際ホテル 和食レストラン もんど岬
name_en: Toba Kokusai Hotel Washoku Restaurant Mondo Misaki
cuisine: 日本料理・海鮮（伊勢志摩の会席／リゾートWashoku、鳥羽湾の眺望）
neighborhood: 鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイング3F・日和山／鳥羽駅徒歩圏
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a kaiseki-style washoku restaurant where wheat is pervasive: soy sauce (shoyu), tempura/fried items, and seasoning all typically contain wheat, and dashi-based dishes are standard. The parent hotel operates a food-allergy intake system (download the 食物アレルギー・お伺いシート and submit by fax/email at least 4 days before the reservation; allergens it can address include wheat 小麦). However, the hotel explicitly states it CANNOT accept requests where trace amounts/cross-contamination (コンタミネーション) could cause severe symptoms, nor cases involving multiple allergens — so it is not suitable for medically strict celiac diners. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or a dedicated gluten-free prep line. Anyone needing strict GF must contact the hotel well in advance and confirm directly; do not assume safety.
vegan_detail: The hotel lists vegetarian/vegan accommodation as available, but this is a seafood-forward washoku/kaiseki venue (signature items are spiny lobster, abalone, and market seafood, plus branded meats). Standard dishes rely on bonito/katsuo and kombu dashi, so hidden fish-derived stock is the norm. Vegan/vegetarian meals would be a special arrangement, not a printed menu — request through the same advance allergy/dietary intake process (submit at least 4 days ahead) and confirm that dashi and seasonings are plant-based. Treat as ask, never assume.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-23-1 鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイング3F
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-23-1 returned [136.839523, 34.488323], inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). Near the northern edge of the box but valid.
seats_text: About 60–72 seats; private rooms available (up to 14 people, room charge ¥11,000).
hours_raw: ディナー 17:30–22:00（ラストオーダー 20:30）。基本的に毎日営業（年に2回程度の不定休）。Monday left as closed only as a conservative placeholder — restaurant is reservation-based and effectively open daily; confirm date directly.
closed_days: 不定休（年2回程度）。ホテル内レストランのため予約状況により変動。
cultural_comfort_note: A polished resort-hotel washoku room used to international guests; reservations and dietary requests are best arranged in advance through the hotel, ideally with a guide or written sheet to convey strict needs accurately.

bio.background: Mondo Misaki is the washoku room on the upper floor of the Toba Kokusai (Toba International) Hotel, perched above Toba Bay with sweeping views of Ise Bay. It carries a 'Resort Washoku' concept and draws on the Ise-Shima region's old role as Miketsukuni (御食国) — a land that historically supplied food to the imperial court.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Ise-Shima seafood kaiseki — spiny lobster (Ise-ebi) and abalone are the signatures, alongside market-fresh fish and regional branded meats (Matsusaka beef, Kumano beef, Ise chicken, Matsusaka pork).
bio.philosophy: The kitchen sources its fish directly at the Tashijima market in Toba, prizing 'the pleasure of rarity' — seasonal catches you won't find in supermarkets — presented in a modern washoku style while honoring the region's food heritage.

anecdotes.text: Chefs travel by boat to the Tashijima market in Toba to personally select the day's seafood, bringing back catches that are only available in season.
The restaurant frames its cooking around Ise-Shima's identity as Miketsukuni, the historic food region that supplied the imperial court.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The hotel states it cannot accept dietary requests where trace amounts or cross-contamination (コンタミネーション) could trigger severe symptoms, nor cases involving multiple allergens — meaning strict celiac-level avoidance cannot be guaranteed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a kaiseki-style washoku restaurant, dishes rely on soy sauce and wheat-containing seasonings; no wheat-free shoyu is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-centered kitchen using bonito/kombu dashi as standard; plant-based meals would be a special off-menu arrangement, so cross-contact with fish-derived stock is a real risk unless explicitly arranged.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel runs a formal food-allergy intake: download the allergy inquiry sheet (食物アレルギーお伺いシート) and submit by fax or email at least 4 days before the reservation; listed allergens include wheat, egg, dairy, soba, peanut, and soy.

positives.text: Vegetarian/vegan accommodation is listed as available with advance arrangement.
A structured advance allergy-sheet process exists, so needs can be communicated in writing before arrival.

caveats: Hotel restaurant inside Toba Kokusai Hotel; dinner-only and reservation-based. Honest limit for celiac diners: the hotel explicitly declines requests where trace/cross-contamination could cause severe reactions and won't handle multiple-allergen cases, so it is 'ask' (not safe by default) for strict GF. Vegan/vegetarian is possible only by special advance arrangement around a dashi-heavy seafood kitchen. Hours treated as irregular (year-round with ~2 unfixed closures); confirm the exact date directly. Chef not publicly named.

lead: ごはんや ふくつぎ
name_ja: ごはんや ふくつぎ
cuisine: 食堂・地元定食（日替わり定食、唐揚げ・味噌カツ・とんかつ等の家庭的な大盛ランチ）
gf_detail: This is a hearty Japanese home-style teishoku (set-meal) eatery built around deep-fried mains — karaage (fried chicken), miso katsu, tonkatsu, and ebi-fry — all of which use wheat-flour batter/breading, so the core menu is not gluten-free. Fried items share a common fryer, meaning heavy cross-contamination risk; there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free fryer. Set meals come with miso soup (dashi typically contains bonito and the miso/seasonings are not wheat-free) and rice. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation has been documented. A celiac diner cannot be assured of a safe meal here; treat as not suitable without direct, careful confirmation with the kitchen.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood — fried chicken, pork cutlet, tonkatsu, ebi-fry (shrimp), and seafood set meals (including oyster/shrimp platters). Set meals include miso soup almost certainly made with katsuo (bonito) dashi, and there is no documented vegetable-only or plant-based option. No vegan accommodation is indicated. Not suitable for vegans; even nominally vegetable sides should be assumed to contain animal-derived dashi or seasoning unless confirmed.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-3-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.841324, 34.482449] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽二丁目３番１号, within the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). Roughly 0.4km from Toba Station — well inside the 0.8km radius.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a small local set-meal eatery occupying a former Chinese-restaurant space, drawing families, students, and local workers.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-20:45 (one blog records evening service to 21:00). Closed Tuesdays.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood set-meal spot frequented by locals; expect Japanese-only menu and service, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help, and dietary requests should be made simply and patiently.
caveats: Dietary information is inferred from the dish lineup (fried mains, seafood, miso-soup set meals) rather than any stated allergen policy — there is no published gluten-free or vegan accommodation. Seat count, payment methods, and reservation policy were not documented on the sources reviewed. Evening closing time varies slightly between listings (20:45 vs 21:00).
neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目（鳥羽駅から約0.4km・徒歩約6分）

bio.background: Gohanya Fukutsugi opened on January 1, 2024 in a renovated space that previously housed a Chinese restaurant called Tonton, a short walk from Toba Station. It quickly became a beloved neighborhood canteen — the kind of unpretentious place where families, students, and local workers in their work clothes all turn up for an honest, filling meal.
bio.specialty: Generous home-style fried set meals — karaage, miso katsu, tonkatsu, and ebi-fry teishoku — known for their big portions. Crowd favorites include the ebi-fry set and the bakumori (mega-portion) miso katsu set, served with free large rice and miso soup.
bio.philosophy: Hearty, great-value comfort food: daily-changing teishoku in regular, large, and 'explosive' portion sizes so everyone leaves full.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over and renovated the former 'Tonton' Chinese restaurant, reopening as a set-meal eatery on New Year's Day 2024.
Locals describe it as a 'gacho-style' teishoku house famous for its bakumori ('explosive portion') set meals, drawing families, students, and workers alike.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are deep-fried (karaage, tonkatsu, miso katsu, ebi-fry) in wheat batter/breading, sharing a common fryer — high cross-contamination risk and no dedicated gluten-free fryer indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese set-meal seasonings (soy sauce, miso, sauces) are used and almost certainly contain wheat; no wheat-free condiments documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat- and seafood-based with miso-soup set meals; dashi almost certainly contains bonito/katsuo and no plant-based options are documented.

cuisine: カフェ・喫茶店・食堂（セルフ式軽食・甘味）
neighborhood: 鳥羽水族館内
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-3-6 鳥羽水族館
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-3-6 returned [136.843826, 34.483772], inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). Matches the Toba Aquarium location ~600m from Toba Station and very close to Nakanogo Station.
seats_text: Cafeteria/food-court style seating with ocean views; counter and table seating available, capacity not specified. First-come, first-served.
hours_raw: Tabelog: 09:30-16:00 daily. (Some third-party listings show 10:00-16:30; aquarium hours apply since it is inside the facility.)
closed_days: Not specified; follows Toba Aquarium, which is open year-round (365 days).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual self-service cafeteria inside the aquarium with picture/photo menus, so ordering is easy, but staff are unlikely to handle detailed allergy questions in English — bring a written allergen note or use the aquarium's allergen ingredient list.
gf_detail: This is a self-service aquarium cafeteria whose core menu is wheat-heavy and not safe for celiacs: Ise udon and soy-sauce ramen are wheat noodles, Japanese curry roux is thickened with wheat flour, and fried items (karaage bowl, etc.) are battered/floured and share fryers. Standard soy sauce and dashi-based broths typically contain wheat. The aquarium does publish a 28-allergen ingredient list (PDF, FY2026) and offers a "low-allergen curry," but this is aimed at school-group meals and is not a guaranteed gluten-free or celiac-safe preparation; there is no dedicated fryer and no information indicating gluten cross-contamination controls. Treat as not suitable for strict gluten-free needs without confirming via the allergen sheet in person.
vegan_detail: A general-audience cafeteria with no vegan focus. The mains (ramen, Ise udon, Japanese curry, karaage bowl, omelet rice/fried rice) typically rely on meat, egg, and fish/katsuo (bonito) dashi, and soy-sauce broths commonly contain bonito stock. No labeled vegan dishes are advertised. A vegan diner would need to verify ingredients against the aquarium's allergen list, and even then options would be very limited (e.g., soft serve / drinks are not vegan).

bio.background: Nagatokan is a long-established Toba institution founded in 1908 (Meiji 41), set in a large building a few minutes' walk from Toba Station and built to welcome travelers and tour groups arriving on the Ise-Shima coast. It draws its catch from the local fisheries, an area historically known for ama (diving) culture, pearl cultivation, and abundant shellfish.
bio.specialty: Tekone sushi — bonito marinated in a sweet-savory soy sauce and folded through vinegared rice, the fisherman's dish of the Ise-Shima coast — served alongside Ise udon, the famously soft, plump wheat noodle dressed in a dark, slightly sweet tare. The kitchen is also known for generous 'teppari' seafood: abalone, scallops, oysters, sea urchin, and spiny lobster.
bio.philosophy: The house word is 'teppari', a Toba dialect expression meaning big, abundant, and luxuriously good — the spirit behind their generous portions and commitment to fresh local sea ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1908 (Meiji 41), Nagatokan has served Toba's coastal cuisine for over a century, with a building large enough to receive tour buses bringing visitors to the Ise-Shima region.
'Teppari' is local Toba dialect meaning large in form and abundant — 'luxurious and delicious' — and gives the restaurant's hearty seafood style its name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-battered tempura (shrimp, spiny lobster) is on the menu and is fried in a shared kitchen; no dedicated fryer or allergen separation is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tekone sushi is bonito marinated in soy sauce, and Ise udon is served in a soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and the page gives no gluten-free soy alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on seafood with bonito-based dashi and tare; even noodle dishes use a fish-based sauce, so plant-only diners cannot rely on the standard preparations.

The shops fry their isoage to order so customers can eat them at their freshly-made best, and the Toba Station branch sits right by the station gates among the small specialty shops and convenience stores travelers pass on their way to the bay.

bio.background: Sea Lounge S.Y.M sits on the second floor of the Toba Marine Terminal, the modern ferry hub on Toba Bay that connects the mainland to the city's outlying islands. It is the kind of relaxed harborside café where island-hoppers pause before or after a boat, watching ferries and fishing craft slide across the water through big terminal windows.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie Prefecture, on Toba Bay

anecdotes.text: Opening at 7:00 a.m., it is one of the few spots near the harbor where you can sit down to a quiet morning coffee and toast while watching the boats before catching an early ferry to the islands.
Beyond breakfast it serves comforting day-to-day plates — curry rice, Ise udon, yakisoba, and fried-chicken sets — the unfussy menu of a neighborhood terminal café.

cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up coffee stand at a major tourist aquarium, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu works fine, though detailed allergy questions in English may be hard.

bio.background: MUSEA is a Toba-based specialty coffee roaster that opened this branch at the entrance of Toba Aquarium in March 2016. The brand roasts beans it describes as carefully selected from around the world at its oceanside facility in Toba, building a small local group of locations that includes its main general store and cafe, this aquarium stand, a branch by Kintetsu Toba Station, and the Hinoyama Lab.
bio.specialty: House-roasted specialty coffee paired with fresh-baked bakery items and soft serve ice cream
bio.philosophy: MUSEA frames itself around 'a special cup of coffee that feels the sea breeze,' pairing self-roasted coffee with carefully made food to enrich a guest's time by the sea.

anecdotes.text: The stand sits right at the aquarium entrance and can be used without buying an admission ticket, and drinks and snacks may be carried inside the aquarium.
MUSEA roasts its coffee at an oceanside facility in Toba and runs several local outposts, leaning on the 'sea breeze' identity of the town.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The food menu centers on wheat-based bakery items (bread, bagels, scones, muffins, brownies, waffles, apple pie, paninis), so flour is handled throughout the small stand and cross-contact is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soft serve, baked goods, and milk-based drinks dominate the menu; no dairy-free or egg-free items are documented.

positives.text: Cards are not accepted but electronic money and QR-code payments are, so a phone-based payment works.

lead: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店
name_ja: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店
cuisine: イタリアン（地元鳥羽の魚介を使ったパスタ・ピザ／昼はランチ、夜はワインとアラカルトのバー）
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅JR口 徒歩約4分
gf_detail: This is a pasta-and-pizza Italian house, so wheat is central to the core menu and there is no indication of any gluten-free pasta, pizza base, or dedicated preparation. No source mentions gluten-free options, allergy handling, or a dedicated fryer. A celiac diner should treat the kitchen as a high-cross-contamination wheat environment; if visiting, confirm directly before ordering, but expect very limited safe options.
vegan_detail: The menu is strongly seafood-forward (Toba turban-shell/sazae pasta, octopus pizza, oyster cream pasta, pescatore), and no source confirms a dedicated vegetarian or vegan dish. Italian kitchens like this typically offer a tomato or aglio-olio style pasta that could be adapted, but hidden animal stock, cheese, butter, or anchovy is likely and unverified. A vegan diner must ask in advance whether a plant-only pasta can be prepared without dashi/bonito, cheese, or seafood. Do not assume any item is vegan as served.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-6-17

seats_text: 26席（1階カウンター10席、2階テーブル席16席）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local Italian spot near the station with Japanese-language menus and staff; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering and any dietary requests much smoother.
caveats: Dietary information is inferred from a seafood-heavy Italian pasta/pizza menu — no source confirms gluten-free or vegan offerings, so both must be verified directly with the restaurant before relying on them. A 500-yen dinner table charge applies in the evening.

bio.background: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店は、鳥羽駅から歩いてすぐの場所にある、おしゃれでカジュアルな雰囲気のイタリアンダイニング＆バー。1階はカウンター、2階はテーブル席という小さな二階建ての造りで、昼はお得なランチ、夜はワインとアラカルトでしっとりと大人の時間を過ごせる、地元に根ざした隠れ家的な一軒です。
bio.specialty: 地元・鳥羽で揚がる魚介を生かした料理。サザエのパスタ、タコのピザ、牡蠣のクリームパスタ、ペスカトーレなど、港町ならではの旬の海の幸を白ワインに合わせて楽しめます。
bio.philosophy: 地元食材へのこだわりを軸に、鳥羽の海の恵みを季節感のあるイタリアンに仕立てる。
roles: イタリアンダイニング

anecdotes.text: ランチは約10種類のパスタが中心で、見栄え・味・ボリュームのバランスが評判。鳥羽の旬魚介を使った一皿が並びます。
おしゃれでカジュアルな鳥羽の隠れ家として、夜はワインとアラカルトで大人の雰囲気を楽しめると紹介されています。

cuisine: 寿司（町寿司・伊勢湾の地魚）
gf_detail: This is a traditional neighborhood sushi counter where soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is central to the meal, and an omakase-style flow of nigiri and sake snacks is served. No gluten-free accommodation, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen handling is mentioned anywhere. For a celiac, the soy sauce, likely wheat-containing seasonings in simmered/marinated items (e.g., kombu-cured snapper, cooked octopus), and the shared counter prep are unmanaged risks. Treat as not safe unless you can communicate your needs directly and the chef confirms — and even then, expect uncertainty.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused Ise Bay sushi shop built around local fish, shellfish, and sake accompaniments. There is no vegan offering; the rice and most dishes are likely seasoned with fish-derived dashi/seasonings, and the menu is omakase-driven around the day's catch. Not a viable option for vegan diners.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-8-16

seats_text: Counter seating for about 7, plus a small raised tatami area (intimate neighborhood-shop size)
hours_raw: 17:00–22:00; closed Wednesday and Thursday (定休日：水・木). Opening time listed as 17:00 on the tourism association page.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter run by an elderly couple who chat warmly with guests, but it's a Japanese-language local sushi shop with no signs of English support or allergy handling, so some Japanese (or a translation tool and patience) will help a lot.
caveats: Cash only. Evenings only (from 17:00), closed Wednesday and Thursday. Reservations are accepted and recommended given the very small counter. Smoking is allowed at all seats. Budget is roughly 10,000 yen per person for the full omakase experience. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is indicated.
neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目 (about a 10-minute walk from Toba Station)

bio.background: Tasuke is a longstanding neighborhood sushi shop near Toba Station, opened roughly 46 years ago (as of 2023) and run by a husband-and-wife pair. The first-generation owner was born in 1947, and the couple have kept the place small and personal for decades, serving locals — including the area's fishermen, who are regulars.
bio.specialty: Omakase-style sushi and sake snacks built entirely around the day's catch from Ise Bay — an ever-changing lineup that has included Ise spiny lobster, black abalone, flatfish, squid, horse mackerel, ark shell, kombu-cured red snapper, octopus, and mackerel, served over traditional, slightly sweet white sushi rice.
bio.philosophy: A small two-person shop that aims to send travelers home with a warm memory of their Toba trip, centering everything on local Ise Bay seafood and unhurried, friendly hospitality at the counter.

anecdotes.text: The owner and his wife serve guests while trading jokes, keeping the mood relaxed and personal; he once quipped that he wouldn't hand the shop down to his son over concerns about appearances.
Local fishermen are among the regulars, and a visitor described the run of Ise Bay fish here as 'an Ise Bay paradise.'
The shop describes itself as a small place where a couple serve customers with their Toba trip memories in mind.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi counter, soy sauce is integral to the meal and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is centered entirely on Ise Bay fish and shellfish with sake accompaniments; no plant-based or vegan items are offered.

cuisine: 和菓子・かりんとう専門（テイクアウト土産）
gf_detail: This is a karintō (deep-fried sweet) specialty shop whose core product is dough made from Mie-grown wheat flour (Ayahikari variety) deep-fried in rice oil. Wheat is the primary, unavoidable ingredient in essentially every product. There are no gluten-free options and this is not a safe stop for celiac diners. The shop is takeout-only with no kitchen accommodations.
vegan_detail: Products are wheat-dough karintō fried in rice oil, with many plant-based flavors (Ise tea, sesame, brown sugar, aonori seaweed, mandarin, okara). However at least one signature flavor is 煮干し/いりこ (dried sardine/anchovy salt), which is not vegan, and other flavorings are not confirmed. Because multiple flavors share frying and production, a vegan should ask which specific flavors are free of fish/animal ingredients before buying; some flavors appear plant-based but this is not verified per-item.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-6-19
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.844437, 34.484509] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-6-19; within the Toba Station target box and ~280m from the station, consistent with the listed walking distance.
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽一番街・ミキモト真珠島そば）
seats_text: テイクアウト専門・客席なし（No seating; takeout only）
cultural_comfort_note: A small local souvenir shop where staff likely speak mainly Japanese; pointing at flavors works fine for a quick takeout purchase, but expect little English.
caveats: Takeout-only souvenir/snack shop, not a sit-down eatery. Core product is deep-fried wheat-flour karintō — unsafe for celiac/gluten-free diners. Vegans should verify per-flavor since at least one variety uses dried fish (anchovy/sardine).

bio.background: 伊勢志摩かりんとー was launched in August 2016 by みえづくし合同会社 (Mie-Dzukushi LLC), a company formed earlier that year by three local Toba business owners who jokingly called themselves the 'おじさん' (the uncles). Watching younger entrepreneurs revitalize empty storefronts, the trio decided to start something of their own to champion Mie's regional ingredients.
bio.specialty: Karintō (traditional deep-fried sweets) made from Mie-grown Ayahikari wheat flour and okara, fried in locally produced rice oil, sold in a range of regional flavors such as Ise tea, sesame, brown sugar, aonori seaweed, and Yamato-tachibana.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Mie Prefecture's local produce — Mie wheat, Tamaki-cho okara, regional rice oil and seaweed — in a familiar nostalgic snack, with minimal use of chemical seasonings, as a form of local revitalization.
roles: 吉水理裕 (Masahiro Yoshimizu) — co-founder, runs Edion Yoshimizu electronics store
岩佐政徳 (Masanori Iwasa) — co-founder, runs Iwasa Construction
森下功一 (Koichi Morishita) — co-founder, runs the Morishita sake shop

anecdotes.text: The three founders were spurred to act when they felt the local young entrepreneurs were silently asking, 'So what are you uncles going to start?' — and decided 'やったろうやないか!' (let's give it a go!).
The karintō are made with Mie's Ayahikari wheat, okara from Tamaki-cho soybeans, and rice oil from Kuwana — said to be the birthplace of rice oil in Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire product line is wheat-based karintō; wheat flour is the main ingredient of the dough, so there is no gluten-free option and contamination is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavors include a dried-sardine/anchovy (煮干し/いりこ) salt variety, which is not vegan; multiple flavors share the same frying and production, so cross-contact between fish and plant-based flavors is possible.

positives.text: Ingredients are simple and locally sourced (Mie wheat, Tamaki-cho okara, rice oil, regional seaweed/sesame/tea) with minimal chemical seasonings, making the ingredient list relatively transparent.

lead: ホルモン たくちゃん
name_ja: ホルモン たくちゃん
cuisine: ホルモン・鉄板焼（焼肉）
gf_detail: This is a hormone (offal) and teppanyaki grill house. The signature flavor is a house miso-based tare (dipping sauce), and Japanese miso and the soy sauce typically used at such grills almost always contain wheat, making them unsafe for celiacs. Assorted organ meats, chicken, and cabbage are grilled on a shared griddle, so cross-contamination from marinades and other diners' food is unavoidable. No dedicated fryer or gluten-free preparation is indicated, and no allergen information was published. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on grilled beef offal (hormone), harami, mino, heart, liver, and chicken cooked on a teppan. There are no plant-based mains, and even the sides and noodle dishes are grilled with meat on the shared griddle. The miso tare may also contain animal-derived dashi. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-22-23

seats_text: Small local grill with table seating (a 6-seat large table and a 12-seat area) plus counter seating; parking for about 4 cars plus a lot across the street.
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目 (Toba 1-chome)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, locally loved family-run grill where staff cook at the table; expect a Japanese-speaking environment, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help.
caveats: Hours are listed only as a lunch start time (11:30~) and dinner 17:00-22:00, so a possible midday break is not documented; the daily ranges are best-effort and should be confirmed by phone (080-9981-0453). No published budget, payment, or allergen information.

bio.background: A casual hormone (offal) and teppanyaki grill in central Toba, run by the local personalities known as the Real Brothers, who are familiar from YouTube and TikTok. The shop opened in January 2023 and has quickly become a cherished neighborhood spot.
bio.specialty: Hormone yakisoba - assorted offal (harami, upper mino, hormone, heart, liver), chicken thigh, and cabbage grilled by staff right on your tabletop griddle, eaten with the house special miso-based tare.
bio.philosophy: Hormone grilling as the heart of a hands-on, generous teppan experience, with big cuts of meat and the entertainment of tableside cooking.

anecdotes.text: The brothers grill the meat and vegetables for you on the teppan, and a signature miso-based dipping sauce is set on the table to season to taste.
Known as a locally cherished establishment with generous, large cuts of meat and a lively atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Assorted offal, meats, and cabbage are grilled together on a shared teppan, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature flavor is a house miso-based tare; Japanese miso and the soy sauce used at such grills typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free version is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is grilled on the same meat-covered teppan with offal and chicken; there are no separate plant-based preparations.

cuisine: 焼肉・ホルモン（牛タン・希少部位、松阪牛、個室あり）
neighborhood: 鳥羽・中之郷
