seats_text: Very small counter-style shop, around 6 seats; private room available.
hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday 18:00–21:00 (one source lists evening service until 22:00); closed Mondays. Dinner only.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional, owner-run yakiniku counter where the proprietor chats warmly with guests; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, and there is no sign of English service.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakiniku and hormone (offal) specialist built around Matsusaka beef, beef tongue, yukhoe (raw beef), and grilled offal. There is no vegan menu, and even side items like kimchi and the tare/dipping sauces would carry animal- and bonito/dashi-derived risks. Not a viable choice for vegan diners.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published and no accommodation is indicated. As a yakiniku shop, the dipping tare and any soy sauce almost certainly contain wheat, and a single shared grill plus a 6-seat kitchen means high cross-contact risk. Plain salt-grilled cuts (e.g., tan-shio) could in theory be lower-risk, but this is unconfirmed and would require a careful conversation in Japanese; treat as not safe for celiacs without direct verification.

bio.background: Senmonten is a small, traditional yakiniku and hormone (offal) shop tucked into the Toba waterfront, a short walk from both Nakanosato and Toba stations. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run counter where the master (taisho) greets regulars and visitors alike with bright, easy hospitality.
bio.specialty: Premium Matsusaka beef grilled over the table — the special loin (特選ロース) and kalbi (short rib) are the signature cuts, described as melting in the mouth, alongside beef tongue (牛タン), yukhoe, and offal such as tecchan.
bio.philosophy: Serve top-grade local beef simply and generously in a calm, homey, Japanese-style room where families and children are warmly welcomed.

anecdotes.text: The interior is calm and traditional Japanese in style, and the proprietor's bright hospitality is part of the draw; families with children are very welcome.
Beyond the beef, the shop is proud of award-winning rice and house kimchi served as sides.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small yakiniku shop (around 6 seats) with table grilling, implying shared grills and surfaces and high cross-contact risk; no GF protocol is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku service relies on tare dipping sauce and soy sauce, which in Japan typically contain wheat; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on beef, beef tongue, yukhoe and offal; sides include kimchi and rice, with no plant-based or dashi-free options indicated.

caveats: No official website found; hours confirmed across Tabelog and Hot Pepper (18:00–21:00, closed Mondays), though one blog lists service to 22:00. Seat count (~6) comes from a single blog and is approximate. No published allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information — dietary assessments are inferred from the meat-centric concept and standard yakiniku practice and should be verified directly before a celiac or vegan visit.

lead: 御食事処 花さんご
name_ja: 御食事処 花さんご
cuisine: 郷土料理・洋食・カレー（メイちゃんのカレー、ロコモコ、ローストビーフ等）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a casual aquarium food court-style eatery whose signature dishes are Japanese-style curry rice, loco moco, and roast beef plates. Japanese curry is made from a wheat-flour roux and is not safe for celiacs; loco moco gravy and any teriyaki/demi-glace sauces also typically contain wheat. No allergen menu, ingredient sourcing, or cross-contamination information is published. A dedicated celiac should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm specifics on-site (and language/staff capacity to do so is unverified).
vegan_detail: No vegan offering confirmed. The menu centers on meat dishes (roast beef, loco moco) and curry; the curry is described as appealing to children but there is no statement that it is meat-free or animal-product-free, and Japanese curry roux commonly contains animal fats/dashi. The broccoli/potato garnish on the otter-shaped "Mei-chan's Curry" is decorative, not a standalone vegan plate. No source documents a vegetable-only or plant-based option. The earlier "veggie-leaning dish" framing is not supported by any page reviewed.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-3-6（鳥羽水族館内）
geocode_note: GSI address search for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3-3-6 returned [136.843826, 34.483772], inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852) and within ~0.8km of Toba Station. Matches the Toba Aquarium location.
neighborhood: 鳥羽・中之郷（鳥羽水族館内）
seats_text: Seat count not published; in-aquarium restaurant with large tank-view seating, fully non-smoking.
closed_days: 年中無休（水族館の営業に準ずる）。夏休み期間（8月）は9:30-17:00に延長。
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, family-oriented restaurant inside Toba Aquarium with no published English support or allergen handling, so a GF/vegan diner should not rely on it for safe dietary accommodation and is best served by guidance before visiting.

bio.background: Hanasango is the in-house restaurant of Toba Aquarium in Mie Prefecture, where guests dine alongside a large coral-reef tank so fish drift past the windows during the meal.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the otter-shaped 'Mei-chan's Curry,' modeled on Mei, the aquarium's beloved sea otter, served with broccoli and star-cut potato; it is offered at lunchtime only, while the Walrus Parfait is served outside lunch hours.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into playful, photogenic, family-friendly plates designed from a 'women's-eye' perspective, alongside hearty options like loco moco and roast beef.

anecdotes.text: The signature curry is shaped after the aquarium's idol sea otter 'Mei,' and is offered only during lunchtime, while the Walrus Parfait is sold during non-lunch hours.
Diners eat while watching marine life in a large reef tank, giving families a relaxed break inside the aquarium.

caveats: In-aquarium restaurant; access requires aquarium admission. Hours follow the aquarium (10:00-16:30 most of the year; 9:30-17:00 in August), with full food service roughly 10:00-15:00 and only light refreshments 15:00-16:00 (L.O. 16:00). No published GF/vegan or allergen information; signature dishes (Japanese curry, loco moco, roast beef) are wheat- and meat-based. No reservations.

lead: 四季のご馳走家 味彩
name_ja: 四季のご馳走家 味彩
cuisine: 居酒屋・海鮮（漁港直送の鮮魚、海女の母が獲る貝類、いけす活魚のお造り）
gf_detail: This is a traditional seafood izakaya with no stated gluten-free accommodation. Core preparations rely on wheat-containing Japanese seasonings: sashimi and shellfish are served with regular soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat), and signature dishes such as wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu use soy-based ponzu. Fried items (e.g. fried oysters) and tempura are on the menu and would share a fryer with breaded/wheat-floured items — no dedicated fryer is mentioned. Even the fresh sashimi (the safest-looking option) is normally eaten with shoyu. A celiac diner should not assume safety here; at minimum they would need to bring their own GF tamari, confirm raw fish is plated separately, and avoid anything fried or sauced. No cross-contamination controls are documented.
vegan_detail: A seafood-centric izakaya whose identity is fresh fish and shellfish from local ports and from the owner's ama-diver mother — there is no vegan menu or stated plant-based focus. Japanese izakaya cooking here leans on animal-derived umami: bonito (katsuo) dashi is explicitly used (e.g. wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu), and most simmered/dressed vegetable dishes in this style hide katsuo or fish stock. A vegan would have very little to safely order and must ask in detail about every dish; assume hidden dashi/bonito throughout. Not a destination for vegan dining.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽3丁目9-7
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.842529, 34.48172] for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽三丁目9番7号; within target box and ~0.8km between Toba and Nakanogo stations.
seats_text: About 40 seats, including counter seating and tatami/private rooms on the second floor.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:45–14:00 and 17:00–24:00 (dinner L.O. ~22:45); closed Sunday. Note: the official site lists dinner-only hours of 17:00–23:00 daily, so hours may vary — confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Sunday (official site lists no regular closing day; sources conflict)
cultural_comfort_note: A local Toba seafood izakaya run by an owner-chef and his ama-diver family; warm and welcoming to both residents and visitors, but expect a Japanese-only environment, so basic Japanese or a translation aid helps for ordering and explaining dietary needs.

bio.background: Born in Toba City in October 1970, the owner-chef spent 18 years working in hotels and izakaya kitchens before opening his own place specializing in the freshly caught seafood of his hometown.
bio.specialty: A fresh sashimi platter built from the day's catch and from live fish guests can pick straight out of the in-store tank, alongside shellfish brought in by the chef's mother.
bio.philosophy: To serve guests seafood at its freshest — straight from the local fishing port and the diving grounds.

anecdotes.text: The chef's mother is still an active ama (free-diving fisherwoman) at 72, and she supplies the shellfish for the restaurant.
As a boy he would visit his mother's seaside ama hut and eat the seafood dishes the divers cooked there — an experience he credits with setting him on his culinary path.
Diners can choose live fish from the tank in the shop to be prepared as sashimi to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried oysters and tempura, which in an izakaya kitchen share fryer oil with wheat-breaded items; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and shellfish are served with standard soy sauce, and a signature wakame dish uses bonito-dashi ponzu (soy-based) — typical Japanese shoyu/ponzu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is explicitly used (e.g. wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu); fish stock is likely present in many dishes that otherwise appear vegetable-based.

positives.text: Very fresh raw fish and shellfish straight from the tank and local port means plain sashimi can be eaten without sauce if the diner brings or requests a wheat-free alternative.

caveats: Hours conflict between sources (Tabelog/Hitosara show lunch + dinner with Sunday closed; the official site shows dinner-only 17:00–23:00 with no closing day) — confirm directly before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented anywhere; ratings here are conservative based on this being a standard seafood izakaya. Allergy handling, dedicated fryer, and exact soy-sauce/dashi composition were not stated and should be confirmed with staff.

cuisine: 海鮮・郷土料理（伊勢えび・あわびなど伊勢志摩の海の幸を使った和食）
gf_detail: This is a casual Japanese seafood eatery with no gluten-free program. Signature dishes are gluten-heavy: jumbo shrimp fry / ebi-fry curry (wheat-flour breading, shared fryer with other fried items), and set meals served with miso soup and Japanese sides. Sashimi and grilled dishes are served with standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and Japanese dashi commonly used in simmered/seasoned dishes. No dedicated fryer, no gluten-free soy sauce, and no allergen handling protocol is documented. A celiac should treat this place as not safe without bringing tamari and confirming preparation in person.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around seafood — live Ise lobster, abalone, sashimi, fisherman's rice bowl, and shrimp dishes. Even rice/vegetable items would typically rely on katsuo/bonito dashi and the kitchen's standard fish-based seasonings. No vegan options are advertised and the concept is fundamentally fish-centric, so a vegan diner cannot be reliably accommodated.

bio.background: 和食堂 いせえび sits inside TOBA Pearl Town along the road to the Toba Aquarium, in one of the few spots in Toba where you can eat while looking out over Toba Bay toward Mikimoto Pearl Island and the boats crossing the water.

bio.philosophy: A relaxed, family-friendly place to enjoy the region's signature catch with a sea view rather than a formal fine-dining experience.

anecdotes.text: It is described as one of the very few restaurants in Toba where you can dine while gazing over the bay at Pearl Island and passing ships.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature fried items (jumbo shrimp fry, ebi-fry curry) use wheat-flour breading and would share fryer oil with other fried foods; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and set meals are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free/tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-focused (lobster, abalone, fish, shrimp) and seasoned with fish-based dashi, so no vegan-safe separation exists.

lead: 本家かまどや 鳥羽店
name_ja: 本家かまどや 鳥羽店
cuisine: 弁当・持ち帰り（白米中心のお弁当チェーン）
gf_detail: A branch of a large takeout-bento chain. The base is white rice (国産米), and many items let you swap or choose components, so a plain-rice-plus-careful-side meal is conceivable — but this is NOT a celiac-safe environment without checking. The chain's own allergen notice lists 小麦 (wheat) among the 8 mandatory allergens and warns that tempura items share a common batter (共通の天ぷら衣) and that factory/store production, cooking and plating are shared, so trace cross-contact with wheat is possible. Soy sauce (used in many sides and the standard seasoning) is wheat-brewed; the chain cites the common position that wheat allergen is broken down during soy-sauce brewing, but that does not make a dish certified gluten-free, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF preparation. A celiac diner must consult the chain's per-item allergen search (アレルゲン検索) and confirm with staff before ordering; treat as ask, not safe.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling. As a mainstream Japanese bento chain, the offerings center on fried chicken, fish, pork and egg-based dishes; sauces and simmered sides commonly contain katsuo/bonito dashi, and the shared cooking environment means cross-contact with animal ingredients. A plant-only meal is not offered as such — at best a diner could ask for plain rice plus a vegetable side and verify ingredients, so vegan is ask/effectively limited. Verify dashi and seasoning with staff.

bio.background: 本家かまどや (Honke Kamadoya) is one of western Japan's long-running made-to-order bento chains, known for hot boxed lunches prepared at the shop and built around carefully chosen domestic rice (国産米). The Toba branch is a small local outpost a few minutes from Toba Station, serving commuters, workers and families who want an affordable hot meal to take away — and it also runs a local delivery service.
bio.origin: Western Japan bento chain (本家かまどや)
bio.specialty: Hot, made-to-order bento boxed lunches over white rice, with the option to swap the rice base for takikomi gohan (+¥110) or fried rice (+¥170).
bio.philosophy: Affordable, freshly assembled boxed lunches using selected domestic rice, with a menu built so customers can choose and customize their box.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers local delivery (配達) and notes it will even take orders on its regular closing days by prior arrangement (休業日の予約承ります／要相談).
The chain emphasizes using carefully selected domestic rice: 本家かまどやでは厳選した国産米を使用しております.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The chain's allergen notice states that factory and store production, cooking and plating are shared across products, so an item may pick up trace amounts of allergens (including wheat) not in its own recipe.
Tempura items in some bento are all cooked in a common batter: 一部のお弁当に使用している天ぷらは、全ての食材を共通の天ぷら衣で調理しています。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain notes the common view that wheat allergen is broken down during soy-sauce brewing, so soy sauce need not necessarily be removed for wheat allergy — but this does not make a dish certified gluten-free for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: のり, ちりめんじゃこ and あおさ used in some bento are harvested by methods that also catch shrimp and crab (えび・かに が混ざる漁法), indicating shellfish/animal cross-contact in seemingly plant items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes a per-item allergen search and an 8-allergen disclosure (小麦・そば・卵・乳・落花生・えび・かに・くるみ) and advises confirming current information, as allergen/nutrition data is updated periodically.

positives.text: Base is white rice using selected domestic rice, and the menu allows swapping the rice and choosing components, giving a careful diner room to build a simpler box.
A published online allergen lookup tool exists, so ingredients can be checked per item before ordering.

caveats: This is a branch of a national takeout-bento chain, not an independent or allergen-specialized restaurant; treat all GF/vegan claims as "ask and verify," not safe. The task line listed the area as 鳥羽1丁目, but the confirmed address is 鳥羽3丁目2422 (still ~319m from Toba Station and within the target radius). bio.background: A casual, hidden-gem style izakaya near Toba Station that opened in mid-2019, serving the local fishing town's catch in a relaxed, neighborly setting with private-room seating.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a working port town on Ise-Shima Bay known for its fresh seafood.
bio.specialty: Local fish and seafood: tuna belly (toro), seared sea bream, amberjack (kanpachi), grilled scallops, and seasonal hot pots, paired with a broad selection of Japanese sake, shochu, wine and regional Mie drinks.
bio.philosophy: A friendly opening message — roughly 'we've just opened, thank you for your support, we'll do our best' — captures its earnest, community-minded spirit.

anecdotes.text: Opened in June 2019 with a heartfelt 'just opened, thank you, we'll do our best' greeting to the neighborhood.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional seafood izakaya, sashimi and grilled dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on fish and shellfish, and broths/simmered dishes are very likely made with bonito (katsuo) or fish dashi; assume animal-derived stock throughout.

caveats: No published allergy/dietary information and no official website or detailed menu were found; dietary assessments are inferred from the seafood-izakaya genre and should be confirmed in person. Hours are listed as daily 18:00–23:00 but closures are irregular (不定休), so call ahead. Cash only.

cuisine: Yakitori / izakaya — Ise-dori chicken, local Toba seafood, and Mie sake
vegan_detail: A long-running yakitori and seafood izakaya with no advertised vegan or vegetarian menu. Nearly all dishes are built around chicken, pork, or fish; even the complimentary appetizer is local wakame seaweed and the tsukidashi/small plates lean on fish-based dashi and bonito. Tare (sweet skewer glaze) and most seasonings are soy-sauce based. A vegan diner would need to call ahead and ask directly whether anything (e.g. plain grilled vegetables) can be prepared without animal-derived dashi, broth, or bonito — assume hidden katsuo/fish stock otherwise.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program and no information published about wheat or allergen handling. As a traditional yakitori/izakaya, the core risks are: yakitori tare and most seasonings made with wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu); dashi and broths likely seasoned with soy sauce/mirin; and fried items (the menu includes fried shrimp, crispy potato-bacon, and seared/fried dishes) sharing oil with breaded items — no dedicated fryer is indicated. Shio (salt-only) skewers may exist but cross-contact with tare brushes and shared grill is unconfirmed. A celiac should not assume any dish is safe without a detailed phone conversation in Japanese; treat as ask-only, leaning toward no.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-4-3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the chō-level point for 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-4-3: coordinates [136.840759, 34.482834]. Inside the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852); roughly 200–300m from Toba Station, well within 0.8km.
neighborhood: Toba (near Toba Station / Toba Aquarium), Toba City, Mie
seats_text: About 16 seats: counter seating (around 9 seats) plus a private tatami room for up to 8 people.
hours_raw: 18:00–24:00 (one source lists 17:00–23:30). Closed Sundays (open on Sunday when the following Monday is a public holiday; one source also notes a closure on the third Monday of the month).
closed_days: Sundays (open on Sunday when Monday is a public holiday); one source also reports the third Monday of the month closed.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-oriented family izakaya with counter and tatami seating and no indication of English support, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) goes a long way, especially for explaining dietary needs.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ slightly between sources (18:00–24:00 vs 17:00–23:30; Sunday closure, with possible third-Monday closure) — confirm by phone. No published allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information; all dietary claims here are inferred from the cuisine type and should be verified directly with the shop. Cash only.

roles: Family-run: parents run the izakaya; their son cooks and developed new menu items
bio.background: A long-standing yakitori and seafood izakaya in central Toba, just a short walk from the station and the bay. It is run as a family business: the parents keep the izakaya going while their son helps in the kitchen and runs a sister sushi restaurant nearby. The son trained in Australia before returning to develop new dishes for the family table.
bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a port town whose menu leans on local fish and Mie-prefecture chicken and sake.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled Ise-dori (Ise chicken) skewers — a signature being the Ise-chicken skewer in spicy miso (伊勢とりのぴり辛みそ焼き) — alongside an original seared Spanish-mackerel sashimi with charred skin (さわらの炙り揚げ刺) and an unusually wide range of yakitori.
bio.philosophy: An unpretentious neighborhood izakaya built on Ise chicken, the day's local catch, and Mie sake, with a warm, low-key family character.

anecdotes.text: The complimentary appetizer (tsukidashi) is bright local wakame seaweed, a small nod to the surrounding Toba coast.

Beyond classic skewers the kitchen serves playful originals like 'jagabe' (crispy potato and bacon) and 'tomabe' (cherry-tomato and bacon skewers), reflecting the son's new-menu experiments after training abroad.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional yakitori and izakaya kitchen with fried items on the menu (fried shrimp, seared/fried dishes); no dedicated gluten-free fryer or allergen protocol is indicated, so shared oil and shared grill cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare (skewer glaze) and izakaya seasonings are soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu and complimentary dishes (e.g. wakame tsukidashi, small plates) typically rely on fish/bonito dashi; an almost entirely meat- and seafood-based menu makes hidden animal stock likely. No vegan items advertised.

positives.text: Small family-run counter where the cooks are right in front of you, making it easier to ask directly about ingredients before ordering.

cuisine: 寿司（地元の小さな寿司店）
neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-1-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-1-5 returned [136.841675, 34.482159], within the target box (lat 34.474–34.489, lng 136.835–136.852). About 457m / 7-min walk from Toba Station.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Presents as a small local sushi shop.
hours_raw: Hours not published online.
closed_days: Not published; call ahead to confirm.
cultural_comfort_note: A small independent neighborhood sushi shop with no English-facing presence; basic Japanese and a polite phone call ahead will go a long way.
vegan_detail: A traditional local sushi shop centered on fresh local fish; no vegan offerings are advertised, and sushi-counter staples (dashi, miso soup, egg, and the fish itself) typically rely on katsuo/bonito and animal ingredients. No vegan menu has been confirmed. A vegan diner would need to call ahead and discuss what, if anything, is possible.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published for this shop. As a traditional sushi shop, the default soy sauce (shoyu) almost certainly contains wheat, and miso soup/dashi commonly do as well. Sushi rice (vinegared rice) and most raw nigiri toppings are naturally wheat-free, but tamago (egg), imitation crab, tempura, and any fried items are typical gluten risks. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or allergen protocol. A celiac diner must call ahead, explain wheat allergy clearly (小麦アレルギー), and consider bringing their own GF tamari, since cross-contamination on shared surfaces and brushes is unaddressed.

bio.background: A small, independent sushi shop tucked into the Toba 2-chome neighborhood, a short walk from Toba Station in this fishing-port town on the Ise-Shima coast. Like many shops here, it leans on the abundant local catch that gives the area its reputation for sushi and seafood.
bio.origin: 鳥羽市（三重県・伊勢志摩）

caveats: Very limited online footprint: no published hours, closed days, seat count, menu, photos, or reviews. Existence, address (三重県鳥羽市鳥羽2-1-5), phone (0599-25-2513), and proximity to Toba Station are confirmed; operating status shows no closure/掲載保留 notice but could not be independently verified beyond listings. All dietary guidance is general sushi-shop reasoning, not shop-specific — call ahead.

lead: メインダイニング シーホース
name_ja: メインダイニング シーホース
cuisine: リゾートフレンチ・地元海鮮（伊勢海老・鮑・松阪牛）
neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽国際ホテル）
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-23-1 鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイング 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽1-23-1 returned [136.839523, 34.488323], inside target box and within ~0.8km of Toba Station.
seats_text: 88席（全席禁煙、シェフズカウンターあり）
closed_days: 不定休（年2回）。2026年3月より金曜のランチ営業は休止。
cultural_comfort_note: A polished resort-hotel dining room used to international guests, with reservation systems in English and formal service, so foreign visitors can expect a comfortable experience.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or GF certification. This is classic resort French cuisine (flour-based sauces, butter roux, bread course) so default dishes are not GF. The hotel does accept food-allergy requests via a downloadable allergy sheet submitted at least 4 days before the visit, and lists wheat (小麦) and soy (大豆) among handled allergens. However, the hotel explicitly states it may DECLINE requests where even trace cross-contamination could trigger a severe reaction, or where there are multiple allergies. There is no dedicated fryer and no isolated GF prep area, so a strict celiac should treat this as ask-only and not assume safe accommodation — confirm directly before booking.
vegan_detail: A dedicated Vegan Course (ヴィーガンコース, around ¥12,100) is offered using French technique, and the hotel also markets a vegan-course stay plan. Because the standard kitchen relies heavily on seafood, butter, cream and likely fish/bonito-based stocks, vegans must order the dedicated vegan course rather than asking for modified standard dishes; confirm the course covers all meals and that no animal-derived dashi or butter is used in the vegan plates when reserving.

bio.background: メインダイニング シーホースは鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイングの1階にあり、日本百景にも選ばれた鳥羽湾を眼下に望むリゾートフレンチのメインダイニングです。
bio.specialty: 伊勢海老・鮑・松阪牛をいかしたフレンチのガストロノミーコース（ヴォヤージュ／シグネチャー／テロワール／ガストロノミークなどの段階的なコース構成）。
bio.philosophy: 「まさに水揚げされたばかりの旬の味」を、鳥羽の海を望む空間で味わってもらうこと。サステナブルを意識したコースも用意している。

anecdotes.text: 眼下に広がるのは美しい鳥羽の海で、日本百景にも選ばれた景観の中で食事ができる、と公式に紹介されている。
ディナーは伊勢海老のヴォヤージュ、伊勢海老と鮑のシグネチャー、伊勢海老・鮑・松阪牛のガストロノミークなど段階的なコースに加え、ブイヤベースコースとヴィーガンコースを揃えている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: ホテルは、コンタミネーション（混入）等で極微量でも重篤な症状が発症する可能性がある場合、安全を最優先しご依頼をお受けできないことがあると明記している。専用調理ラインの記載はない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: アレルギー対応では小麦・大豆などを取り扱い対象として挙げているが、フレンチのため小麦は前提的に使用される（パン・ソース等）。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 食物アレルギーがある場合は公式サイトから「食物アレルギーお伺いシート」をダウンロードし、宿泊・予約日の4日前までにFAXまたはメールで送付する運用。
複数の食材にアレルギーがある場合は対応をお受けできないことがある、と明記。

positives.text: フレンチの技法を用いた専用のヴィーガンコースを通常メニューとして提供しており、ヴィーガン宿泊プランも用意されている。
事前申告制ながら卵・乳・小麦・大豆などのアレルギー対応に応じる体制がある。

caveats: GF is ask-only: a hotel French kitchen with no dedicated GF/celiac protocol and an explicit policy to decline trace-cross-contamination cases. Vegan is well supported via a dedicated course (must be pre-ordered). Hours are irregular (2 closures/year; Friday lunch suspended from March 2026). Reservation effectively required; pricing is high-end (dinner ¥12,100-¥38,500).

cuisine: 海鮮・会席（伊勢海老・鮑・岩牡蠣）
neighborhood: 鳥羽市小浜町・鳥羽湾
gf_detail: No gluten-free program found. This is a traditional kaiseki seafood house where dishes are built on soy sauce (typically wheat-containing), dashi, and battered/fried items such as the large prawn tempura noted in guest reports — all standard sources of gluten. No dedicated fryer or celiac-safe preparation is indicated anywhere. A celiac diner should treat the menu as high-risk and would need to arrange a fully custom meal by phone well in advance, with no guarantee it can be met.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The entire concept is seafood kaiseki centered on Ise spiny lobster, abalone, oysters, tuna, yellowtail and sea bream, with Matsusaka beef also offered. Even non-fish courses in this style rely on bonito/kombu dashi and fish-based stocks, so hidden katsuo/dashi is expected throughout. No plant-based or vegetarian option is advertised.
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市小浜町299-39
geocode_note: GSI geocoding of 三重県鳥羽市小浜町299-39 returned [136.835587, 34.495228], and the chō-level fallback (小浜町) returned [136.831268, 34.496712]; both lie north of the target box (lat 34.474–34.489), roughly 1.6–1.8km from Toba Station. However, this is a floating restaurant on Toba Bay reached by boat from a boarding pier near the station, and the listing states approx. 731m from 鳥羽駅. The 小浜町 chō centroid points to the land-based administrative area rather than the over-water dining location/pier, so coordinates are set null due to this over-water ambiguity rather than treating the venue as out of area.
seats_text: Dining in private rooms / tatami座敷; the inn has 13 guest rooms and rooms can be reserved privately. Exact restaurant seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:30–14:00（L.O. 13:00）／無休（lunch dining hours per listing）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional over-water ryotei reached by boat where reservations are made by phone in Japanese; visitors should expect a Japanese-language experience and arrange any dietary needs in advance in Japanese.
caveats: Geocode is unresolved because the venue floats on Toba Bay and the chō centroid sits outside the 0.8km target box, though the listing states ~731m from the station via a boarding pier. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation could be confirmed; the menu is seafood-forward kaiseki and should be considered unsafe for celiac and unsuitable for vegan diners absent direct confirmation. Listed hours are the published lunch dining window; as a ryokan it also serves accommodation meals not reflected here.

bio.background: Kairakuen is a floating ryotei and inn set out on the calm waters of Toba Bay, off the Kobama coast a short way from Toba Station. Guests cross to the over-water building, where rooms open directly above the sea.
bio.specialty: Ise-Shima seafood kaiseki built around live Ise spiny lobster (伊勢海老) and abalone (鮑), alongside seasonal catches such as oysters, tuna, yellowtail and sea bream, with Matsusaka beef available.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the bounty of Ise-Shima eaten as close to the source as possible — over the very water the fish come from.

anecdotes.text: The house signature is 座敷釣り (zashiki-zuri) — guests can drop a fishing line straight from their tatami room window into the bay below, and the catch can be cooked for them or made into a fish-print (gyotaku) keepsake; fishing is also possible from the inn's private pier and raft.
The building floats on the sea off Kobama beach, so the ocean spreads directly beneath the rooms when the windows are opened.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Guest reports mention large prawn tempura, indicating wheat-battered fried items prepared in shared oil; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese seafood kaiseki house, dishes rely on soy sauce and soy-based seasonings, which in Japan are typically brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely on seafood and meat (Ise lobster, abalone, oysters, tuna, yellowtail, sea bream, Matsusaka beef); fish/bonito dashi is expected throughout and no vegan option is offered.

name_ja: かっぱ寿司 鳥羽店
cuisine: 回転寿司（チェーン）
neighborhood: 鳥羽（鳥羽駅 北・国道42号沿い）
address_ja: 三重県鳥羽市鳥羽
geocode_note: Manual add (Greg) — exact coords from Google Maps; ~0.7km N of Toba Station.
cultural_comfort_note: A conveyor-sushi chain with touch-screen ordering (often multilingual) — easy with little Japanese; the practical GF/vegan fallback near Toba Station.
gf_detail: Conveyor-belt sushi: plain nigiri and sashimi (rice + fish) are naturally wheat-free, BUT the standard soy sauce contains wheat (bring your own GF tamari) and the shared kitchen/belt handles tempura, fried items and imitation crab, so cross-contamination is a real risk. No GF program. A celiac can assemble a meal from plain nigiri/sashimi with care — confirm with staff.
vegan_detail: Plant-based picks exist: kappa-maki (cucumber roll), inari (sweet tofu pouch), corn gunkan, natto-maki, edamame, plain rice. Many items use fish/egg/dashi, so a vegan should stick to those — but a light meal is doable.

bio.background: A branch of the nationwide kaiten-zushi (conveyor-belt sushi) chain — by far the most accessible option near Toba Station for a gluten-free or vegan diner, since you order plate by plate and can build a meal from plain nigiri, cucumber rolls, inari and rice.
bio.specialty: À-la-carte conveyor sushi — choosing plate by plate lets a restricted diner pick exactly what works.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared kitchen and belt handle tempura, fried chicken and wheat items; house soy sauce is wheat-based. Plain nigiri/sashimi + your own GF tamari is the safer route.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House soy sauce is wheat-based; bring gluten-free tamari.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many items use fish/egg/dashi; stick to kappa-maki, inari, corn, edamame.

positives.text: A-la-carte format lets you choose exactly what fits — a rare flexible option in Toba.
